speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This conversation with William Morgan took place at his desk at Morgan Brothers in Lewiston on October 28th 1976. Because business was being conducted at the time of the conversation there is a considerable amount of noise on this tape in places. The interviewer was Sam Schrager.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You were just five?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where did you come from?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Kansas City. Right off from Kansas.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: With your folks?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Yeah. My folks sold out from Kansas. I was born what is now the center of Fort Riley. We had a homestead right in the center of the prison at Fort Riley which is 10 miles square. And then we landed up here at Kootenai Lake up on the Kootenai River.

speaker: William Morgan
words: And my dad is sold out there mad at the country of Kansas because of a big thunderstorm. He had quite a farm and he run stock scales and bought and sold stock. The big rainstorm came up one forenoon when he'd received a carload of hogs and the rain soaked the ground. The next morning the cockleburs came up which are poisoned. And poisoned probably all of this carload of hogs. And he just sold out his two thrice and outfits. Everything he had moved out west here. We landed at Kootenai and stayed there that summer while he looked the reservation over here for a homestead. He finally bought a relinquishment. We moved down here that summer and stayed at what was known as the Olde France Hotel. That's quite a historic spot. There you go.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember at that time when you were first there?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Oh yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it quite a place at the time?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Oh yes. My mother and my brother and myself stayed there about two months that summer. While my dad went over and finished getting ready to move up to the prairie, Nez Perce Prairie. We moved to Moeller, Moeller, Idaho.

speaker: William Morgan
words: Which at that time became quite a town. There's nothing there now but the wind there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where is it near now?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Moeller, Idaho. Well it's between Craigmont and Nez Perce. About half way. At that time a man by the name of Moeller had made arrangements and surveyed the railroad from the Clearwater up Little Canyon. Up the Little Canyon. That's taken off at Peck here. Right up that way up to Moeller. And built a town there and at that time there was three saloons, one hotel, a flour mill, a stock receiving station and scales, two general stores and one hardware, church and school.

speaker: William Morgan
words: I don't know what else. But anyhow, after the survey was made by a volumer from Lewiston to Grangeville, up the Culdesac and Craigmont area up to Long Island which is the present road, while he withdrew his building of the railroad. See it was supposed to be headquartered at Moeller. We were three and a half miles north and east of Moeller, the ranch.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So it would have been a good location here?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Oh yeah. You're good at that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where would it have gone right now?

speaker: William Morgan
words: It would have been the easiest road to travel, the railroad. But they had to cross another canyon, the Lawyer's Canyon to get to Grangeville.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that a fight between Moeller and Vollmer to get the railroad by the route that they wanted to?

speaker: William Morgan
words: No, there wasn't much of a scrap to it to involve the Camas Prairie Railroad. I think it was in the Union Pacific just decided to build it. That's all there was to it.

speaker: William Morgan
words: But Vollmer happened to be at the right place at the right time there.

speaker: William Morgan
words: Well, he wasn't because Vollmer himself had built two or three other town sites and none of them survived. He built a town site which is now Craigmont on the northeast side of the highway. The highway cut right through. Then the Ilo, which used to be an old town about two miles west of Craigmont, it folded up and moved to Craigmont, which was later called Craigmont. And then the battle was on.

speaker: William Morgan
words: Who would be the city and what would be the name of it?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Whether it be Ilo or Vollmer. They finally decided on the name of Craigmont.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Neither one. That happened in Troy in that it was originally called Vollmer, but people changed the name. You heard that. Yeah, that's right.

speaker: William Morgan
words: There's a lot of towns up there. At that same time that Moeller was there, there was a town called Frazier. That's due west of Moeller, about four miles. Then there was another town about two miles east of what is now Craigmont called Dublin. U -B -L -I in Dublin. And then this side of there, this side of Craigmont was Rubens and a little town called Gifford and a community called Summit and another community called Lookout. They were all in a row between Craigmont and, well actually in Peck. And that was called Big Canyon.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, when you first moved there, was it all just very beginning of the farming there?

speaker: William Morgan
words: No, they'd farmed two or three years.

speaker: William Morgan
words: I think they had. Because we bought, Dad bought the ranch and there was a binder on it. It wasn't any good, but there was a pair of horses, team horses, left there from a runaway on this binder. They killed the other two horses and these two survived.

speaker: William Morgan
words: One of them was a racehorse and he had cut his barbed wire practically to his neck, right?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Clear to the cord, you can see his neck. And that horse stayed there and worked on the ranch like any other horse. He was thirty years old.

speaker: William Morgan
words: It sure was the dumbest thing I ever saw. He was just a perfect animal. But he had a chuck cut out of his shoulder and neck here that you could put your hands in and put a board across there and never touch it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that a race common in those days?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Oh yes, lots of Cayuse horses, Indian ponies. This wasn't, it was a half Hamiltonian. It got mixed up with the Indian, but there were lots of wild horses. In fact, Chumman and myself, I was thirteen, he was twelve, and we broke one summer, eleven head. Wild horses had come from a man named Hunt, from a little community called Winona. That's up from the Kamiah on the hill. These were wild horses actually. One of them was nine years old and never had a rope on it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were you thirteen?

speaker: William Morgan
words: I was thirteen and my chum was twelve. We broke that eleven head that summer to work and about two or three of them to ride. We worked them that summer. We had three older horses. One of them was actually an old horse and the other two was four year olds. So we had quite a crew.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Could you tell me how you went about breaking them then?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Well, we had no grain, no feed, so we turned them out at night. We fed them grain in the morning, so we kept one horse to ride and we were on the Looking Glass Indian Reservation Ranch right on top of the hill from Kamiah. The McClouds were the people that owned the property, but not the Indian land. This is Indian land. We turned them out at night and go drive them into this corral. Then we'd last do them. We'd build a corral about eight foot high out of timbers that we'd cut out. We'd rope these horses and snub them up to the posts and then we'd get up on the rail and drop the harness down on top of them. We had a stick with a nail in it. We'd reach through and get the belly button and pull it through and fasten that. We had long chains that we'd put on the butt chains. Part of them were butt chains, part of them wasn't, but they were long tugs. We could get far enough back and we wasn't in too much danger of being kicked. But we treated these horses very nicely and never had much trouble. Only they were wild. A hailstorm came up one day while they were in the field and they just turned around to their backs to the storm and they came right over a harrow. Still hit step to them. Never heard of them.

speaker: William Morgan
words: So then we worked them that summer in a plow. We had six head on a two bottom gang and three on a foot burner plow. The two of us changed off. One to the other all the time. We'd get tired of walking. That was in 19...let's see, that was in 19...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did you come by them in the first place?

speaker: William Morgan
words: A man by the name of McLeod that we worked for. It was his son and me. I was his neighbor in Nez Perce School at that time. And he traded a house and lot there in Nez Perce for 13 head of horses. A man by the name of Hunt at Winona. We went over there and drove them across the canyon and then drove them to work that summer. Try that sometime. It sounds like you started pretty young. Well, people were. Kids were men those days at that age. Because I started my first threshing run when I was 11 years old. I cut bands on the horse power. We had seven teams on the big sweep power driven by a tumble rod from the power plant to the thresher. I worked on that when I was 11 and 12. When I was 13 I drove bundle wagon for the threshing outfit. And then the next year, no, two years after that I drove what they call header box. These boxes were going to my little binder.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you think of that time when the threshing outfit of the business?

speaker: William Morgan
words: My idea of it is quite an operation. Well, it was.

speaker: William Morgan
words: Of course, a kid like me, 11 to 13 years old, I was more proud to be in the threshing crew than if I had run 10 touchdowns on the football field.

speaker: William Morgan
words: Kids at that time were trying to be grown up men. It was a different atmosphere, different thoughts, youngsters. When they got about 15 years old those days, as I remembered, they thought they were men. You know, a kid about that age, he thinks he knows a lot, but he finds out later he didn't. And that's the way I was too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that threshing crew mostly local men from the area?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Oh yes, mostly.

speaker: William Morgan
words: What they usually done, that was a small rig, 28 inch separator, and there was about 16 or 17 in the crew. But it was mostly neighbors and their sons, or nephews or whatever it might be. People lived in the neighborhood. They did hire some outside people.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And did it just thrash the neighborhood?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Or did it go outside?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Oh well, probably within a distance of maybe 5 or 6 miles. Did it have a cook wagon with, or did you... Not at first. Not when we had the horsepower rig. We didn't. We had the horsepower rig two years, and then they bought an engine, and then we had a cook house.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why did that make any difference?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Well, first place it was faster and they had more horses to mow with. And the bundle wagons, usually they'd leave the bundle wagons, a couple of them, and hook the teams from them onto the cook house. That was the main thing to get moved.

speaker: William Morgan
words: The cook house.

speaker: William Morgan
words: Later on I became water butt for the steam rig, and it was my job to move the cook house. And supply water for the thrashing outfit, the horses and the steam engine.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you try to be careful when you moved the cook house? It didn't matter a lot, you know?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Oh well, yes. You had to be careful because you were going across plowed ground. I think all the stuff they had... Well, they didn't have much to cook with. It was pretty well taken care of. We never had any trouble. I never had a bit of trouble. I hauled the cook house for three years. I never had any trouble at all, except I had an old bulky horse once in a while. It'd get too heavy and eat bulk. But I knew how to handle it, so I got moved on.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How much teamwork was there on a crew like that? Did the guys have to work really close together to coordinate things real close to get things done?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Oh yes, pretty much so. We had five bundle wagons, and that took from two to three horses on each one. So that'd be ten, about twelve horses there. And there's fourteen horses on the power, on the horsepower. So that'd be twenty -six head there, and the water team was four. That's thirty head. That's quite a bunch of horses on the thrashing outfit. And nobody wanted the thrashes when it rained because they had to feed them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: As long as it rained?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Well, yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Stayed there?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did that happen very much?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Oh, yes.

speaker: William Morgan
words: And then, oh I forget what year it was. It rained quite a little bit in the fall. And it cost some of the farmers a little bit of money. Of course, it didn't cost them money, only they couldn't make it. They couldn't sell it because the horses ate it up. And then they had to feed the men. Of course, a lot of them went home.

speaker: William Morgan
words: But what extras they did hire?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Well, they usually drew some of their pay and went to town, celebrated for a day or two.

speaker: William Morgan
words: So it wasn't too expensive as far as feeding the people is concerned.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How much chance for socializing with the neighbors during that time? In the evenings and that kind of thing? Was there much?

speaker: William Morgan
words: No. You just, when you got through one of them days, you went to bed right now. You just simply worn out, that's all. There's no socializing very little. Hard work. You just stayed there and slept in the haystacks. Particularly that to do it. Someone wants to talk to you about meat grinders.

speaker: Unknown Speaker
words: You want me to have them call back?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How long a day would that be then? How long a day?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Well, we was up at five and you're supposed to have your horses harnessed and fed before you went to the cookhouse for something to eat. Or the house that you're working at. And you started a machine at seven and some of them got started at six. The horsepower rigs, the horses couldn't take it. They'd usually go away until about seven. The steam engines, they could get off at about six. Well, unless there's somebody that's supposed to call you back from long distance, it's not about the grind. It's different.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And then how long you would go? Would you go right until sunset?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Usually about six o 'clock. But that's sunset then.

speaker: William Morgan
words: So you worked really hard. I mean, hard enough to really do you in, but you know.

speaker: William Morgan
words: Well, it was hard work because all your jobs were not simplified. When you worked on the sacking, sewing sacks on the Thrasher outfit, you handled every one of those sacks. You sewed it up, picked it up, and carried it over to the pile. When you carry 180 sacks a day, that's sewing, jigging, jot them down, you know. That's not for... A lot of small fellows did it, but they were tough. They knew how to do it. They knew how to put it up on their knees. Pick it up. Take these sissies you got here now in these colleges. I don't think one of the biggest football teams they got there. Players could do it. You don't work. You don't know what work is. Thank you very much.

speaker: William Morgan
words: What?

speaker: William Morgan
words: You get up in the morning, you take care of your horses, feed them, take care of yourself as best you can. Maybe you shave, but you don't. Cold, kick the snow off your blankets or your frost. You don't wash your face very much either. You just gotta...

speaker: William Morgan
words: Well, I worked several years on the tressers. The things that bothered us those days was building roads. Because in those days, there was no roads. So every man by law had to pay a poll tax. And you could work out your poll tax with a team of horses. And when I was hired out, it was usually my job to take the boss's team and go out and take his place on the road. And we usually had scrapers, what they called two horse or four horse Fresno's.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know what they are?

speaker: William Morgan
words: You better explain it to me, Fresno. Fresno is a long scraper. It's about five foot long, I'd say. It's about six. And two horses hits each end. And a big long iron handle. You drove these four horses the best you could up to where you was going to load. You take hold of this iron and lift it up and scoop. Scoop up the dirt, rocks, or whatever it was, and then you pull it back down. Now if you think that's easy, just try one. Especially, it is fairly easy if your horses are trained to pull even. But the worst thing I got into, I had two big black mares, weighed about 1400 pounds apiece, and two little mule jinnies. And they weighed about 850. And they were on one end of this scraper and the big horses were on the other end. And when the little mules would feel that bite of that scoop, they'd pull ahead real quick, throw the weight and all the load on the big team. As soon as they got the thing loaded, which therein wasn't loaded, they backed up and went right along as a team. You say a mule ain't smart, you try it. Work with them a while. And this big iron bar would come around and hit me if I was foolish enough to stand in front of it, break your leg, break your bones or anything. Because the weight on that stuff would come in that big long iron about 5, 6 foot long. Hit you there, those teams pulling against you. There's a leverage there to just knock that tire out of you. Anybody that's ever drove a Fresno knows what I'm talking about.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How much of that could you do in a day?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Well, we worked about 10 hours. I think that was the day then, 10 hours on the road.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: On a stretch of road, how long would you have you put in for your poll tax? How many days of work was that?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Well, it depends on how much land you had. There's so much an acre. I don't know what the assessment was, but you assessed so many dollars. And then you got so much a day for a man and his team. Credit.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that how most people did it, was working out on the road?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Oh, a lot of them did, yes, because they didn't have any money. Usually the work on the roads were done in the late spring. And by that time the winter's over, you didn't have any money, that's all. You just didn't have any. So they worked out, most of it, and that's how these roads all over this country were built, working out their poll tax.

speaker: William Morgan
words: The old Kamiah grade from the Indian Reservation there, looking glass and the cash -cash place, down to Kamiah was built by that kind of labor. You get out there and handle a pick and a shovel. If you don't know how to handle a pick or a shovel or a sledgehammer, you got an education coming. I got one. I'd slam that sledgehammer down with all the might I had, and I'd slam it down and I didn't give any with it. My wrist was just almost paralyzed. After about two or three days, I'd let the sledgehammer do its own hitting and I'd hold it loose. It didn't hurt then.

speaker: William Morgan
words: You work on those roads like the men used to do, and that was the job. That road from Nez Perce to Orofino. If you have a good over -dare lately, you'll see what I mean. That original road's still there. And the one from Nez Perce to Greer, there's another one.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there much employment in that for the men?

speaker: William Morgan
words: I mean, besides this coal tax business, there's no regular road jobs that the men can get. No.

speaker: William Morgan
words: There wasn't enough taxes. Taxes were very limited. About one load of wheat paid our taxes on our place. Well, most days, all the ranches up there were 160 acres.

speaker: William Morgan
words: Now they're about 2 ,000.

speaker: William Morgan
words: One man does more work now than the whole neighborhood ever did before. I got called in here without there.

speaker: William Morgan
words: So it's a lot of difference between living now and living then. Just a hell of a lot.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the neighborhood like when you were growing up there? Was there much of a real neighborhood right there where you lived?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Well, yes. There was a man and a family on every 160. Almost a family on every 160 acres.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did that mean that you were very close to the neighbors?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Well, half a mile.

speaker: William Morgan
words: A quarter section of 160 acres is half a mile. So you're half a mile from your closest neighbor. You may have a few more around you.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, how much did that mean you associated with them?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Oh, not too much. He didn't associate maybe once in two months, maybe. On the Sundays. Very little evening associations. There's practically none. The nearest we had to associations was one of the first telephones. My dad helped run the line from Peck to Moeller. And it was run on the barbed wire fences. You'd follow a half a mile of fence along the quarter section, 160 acres. If there's a gateway, you went up over it, across, and down. And the cattle was always breaking the fence down here to there, so your telephone service was not very good. Because maybe down the road eight miles, somebody run into the fence or something.

speaker: William Morgan
words: No connection. No connection. Now our association then, my dad was quite progressive in some ways. We had one of the first phonographs. One of these Edison's with the round records. He ate along about that big around. One of these big horns that came out. And at a certain time in the evening, they would give a ring on the telephone crank. And you could just hear the receivers click, click, click all up and down the line. And we would turn on that phonograph into the telephone. And we'd have the preacher and the bear, and some violin, maybe, or tunes like Devil's Dream, or the old Irish Wash Woman, or Soldier's Joy, or some of those old time tunes. They'd be played for the whole line to listen. That time you couldn't call anybody because everybody had to vote.

speaker: William Morgan
words: And that's about as much socializing as you did, excepting about four miles apart. You'd have a schoolhouse about four or five miles. Maybe closer than that. And in this community with one family on every hundred and sixty acres, you'd have about fifteen families to a schoolhouse. And you'd have what was known as literary society. You put on your own plays, debates, musical programs, religious programs, and everything like that. Box socials. That was what socializing was done those days. I remember my dad and mother had a play that they had made up pretty much themselves, although they got the principal part of it out of a magazine. Slow Train Through Arkansas. You had the old passenger coach, and the kids would train along behind the old maid with her bird in a cage, and all that kind of stuff.

speaker: William Morgan
words: And this slow train through Arkansas, it would stop. There was a cow on the track, or maybe some wagon crossing the railroad. The train would stop and let the wagon cross. Or if it was a little town or somebody lived there that was kind of important or something, why they'd stop and let him off. Or pick up a passenger along the slow train through Arkansas. You rode it just like a streetcar used to be here. You'd probably go thirty miles in a day, maybe fifty.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But did they put this on as a play?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they act the parts?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Oh yeah, of a man and his wife and his family.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: On a train?

speaker: William Morgan
words: On the train. You had your box lunch, and the kids smeared jelly all over the seats, and all that kind of stuff.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you kids act in it too as the kids?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Oh yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And then you put it on for the other families that were there?

speaker: William Morgan
words: There were all kinds of things like that. Debates.

speaker: William Morgan
words: Which came first, the chicken or the egg? Which is the most important, a mopstick or a dishrag?

speaker: William Morgan
words: I made you get into it Doug. Argue all one evening.

speaker: William Morgan
words: They choose up sides, two on the side to lead the debate, and then anybody else could chum in on either side. First thing you know, the whole audience would be one side or the other.

speaker: William Morgan
words: And they'd tell the damnedest stories you ever heard. About, well for instance, a broomstick or a dishrag. Well this old cow had swallowed the dishrag and couldn't get it on down, so the broomstick was most important because they pushed it on down with a broomstick. All such things as that. You know, that was socializing in the old days. And that was not here, that was all over. I've heard of having the literaries up around the ridges of Moscow, which were like, you know, walking there. Oh yeah, well around Pine Grove and out towards Troy and Deary and Southwick and Leland and Cavendish, all those places. But I've never heard an argument for the dishragging.

speaker: William Morgan
words: Oh well, there were a lot of other ones too, but I just happened to think of that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they ever have any political debates or things like that?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Not maybe political, like Democrat or Republican, but over the issues of the day.

speaker: William Morgan
words: Not very much. Not too much. I heard Senator Borah in some of his political speeches, but I was then about eighteen.

speaker: William Morgan
words: That was quite a bit later. They wouldn't do it, say, like in a debate in a schoolhouse. You take one side and then take the other. Once in a while we'd have a religious debate, but that was kind of dangerous.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What kind of question could you debate in religion?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Well, some of them got pretty warm.

speaker: William Morgan
words: They'd get their funny ideas, you know. Most of them were atheists, didn't believe in religion. I know we had one neighbor that was an atheist. He didn't believe in religion. And then a preacher came onto that prairie, which is about seven or eight churches over that Nez Perce prairie. He'd challenge them all to debate, anytime, anyplace. And he'd read the Bible too, he claimed three times. In all the paragraphs marked, he was dangerous. The preacher couldn't hardly, he had to be pretty good to handle it at all. Yet he was as fine a man as you ever saw. He was a perfect gentleman. But he was off on that one thing. And boy, he was off too.

speaker: William Morgan
words: He'd take anybody on.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he declare himself to be an atheist?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Did he say... No, no.

speaker: William Morgan
words: No.

speaker: William Morgan
words: He was that next to a neighbor.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did he present himself? Did he say he just had some questions about it?

speaker: William Morgan
words: He just made the other thought out of line.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you ever see him debate a preacher?

speaker: William Morgan
words: I wasn't very old then. I was about 12. No, I was about 10. About 10. But he'd pretty well read. But he was off on some of these statements, like, naturally it's pretty hard to debate religion. Because you can, as he said, he could prove anything in religion. Either side. Right, you could disprove it too. Yeah. So that's the kind of fellow he was. He was just a fellow who would get up and preach a sermon and tear it to pieces. This is as sure as the sun came up. He didn't care what religion was, what faith it was.

speaker: William Morgan
words: People like your father didn't mind and he'd put up with him and listen to him. Oh, well, yeah, well, the neighbors knew him and paid no attention. He'd get a big kick out of it, laughing at him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he get worked up?

speaker: William Morgan
words: No. No.

speaker: William Morgan
words: No, perfect gentleman.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would you say there were a lot of people out there that weren't believers at the time?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Well, in those days there was. Lots of them. Most of them was because they hadn't had contact with the tracts, not be in the Bible, the good book, or history. Which all three of them go together. And a lot of these roughnecks, see, these three desperadoes in this territory. One time it was reported they had 5 ,000 shinymen up at Pierce, Idaho. They saw I liked a copy of their invoice. They claimed that they found them late till the snow goes off so they could find the dead ones. And all stories like that. It was pretty rough. This McGregor outlaws shot some people up east and south of Grangeville. And they got as far as Lewiston here and they trailed them. And one man from Lewiston followed them into California and caught them. And they brought them back here and hung them. Three of them.

speaker: William Morgan
words: The roads, speaking of roads, if I'm going to go, the roads, for instance, from the Nesb Craigbound out west to Forrest and then down to Soldiers Meadows down to Waugh and then down to Lewiston. And that was rough coming down those hills and just rougher going up too. They used to tell about cutting a tree down on top of the hill and tying it on behind the wagons to hold them back. Horses, a team of horses couldn't hold them. It was steep. Same way going up there, they put what they call rough locks.

speaker: William Morgan
words: You ever see a rough lock?

speaker: William Morgan
words: No, but I've heard of them. Well, you take a wheel like this and an axle through here and a chain come down here or wire or whatever it is and this block set across the back. When your wagon starts back, it loses this chain and this block that sits on the ground and the wheel runs up against it. In other words, it drug along all the time on the ground. The minute you stopped your horses, the load backed against that block.

speaker: William Morgan
words: Locked it. So they all always going up the hill, they had a rough lock. Probably had to stop a lot to pump the horses. Oh yeah, you couldn't go about 100 yards. About those steep spots, you're lucky if you got 100 yards. And a team of horses pulled them all they could and uphill 100 yards is quite a little ways to run without any wind. Coming downhill the same way, we had a rough lock coming downhill. Which is a piece of metal about the size of my hand cupped this way and the wheel backed into it there. Well, that thing was fast up here to the bed.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: To the bed?

speaker: William Morgan
words: The bed of the wagon.

speaker: William Morgan
words: You loosen that about 3 inches or 4 and the wheel would run into this rough lock. Shoe, iron shoe and that would drag. The wheel wouldn't turn down, that's the way it went down the hill. There's a sled.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there another break besides that?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Oh yes, sure. When you put a rough lock on, the wheel didn't turn at all. There was some danger with that too because sliding into the sleet was a level and it would slide off to the lower side. With the rough lock on, sometimes you'd have to loosen that much of the wheel to get back over to us where the power was.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would people's breaks fail?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Sure, you betcha. Lots of horses been killed.

speaker: William Morgan
words: You betcha. Up here when I took a trip from Skagway to Whitehorse in Canada, they had a stretch of road there, a railroad built on there. And they claimed that there was a thousand horses that died in that stretch. The main reason was they worked them until they died with no food. That was the main thing. A lot of killing. And they just worked them until they died to get another field. No feed, no way to feed them. No, 10, 20 foot deep and all that stuff. Same with men up there. In that section of the country, the bank or anything else, he was just a human being, that's all. His money, nobody takes money.

speaker: Unknown Speaker
words: Did any oval canners end, like the old time boilers?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Something goes there. This is 30 miles beyond the Arctic Circle. That's up at Katzavu and Roanoma. That's where the big gold rush was in the Yukon. Of course the Yukon was in Canada, but the river flows down through that section.

speaker: William Morgan
words: You know, when you were talking about religion, it occurred to me to ask you about revivals.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do they have them up there?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Oh yeah, you betcha. Lots of revivals. They had revivals meetings all over. And then later on it turned into Chautauqua. They had some religious meetings in that too. That was supposedly an education deal. Had all the good speakers that got kicked out of Congress or something. And the singers and musicians and everything, Chautauquas. And that was popular for about three or four years. The community was guaranteed, but it got...

speaker: William Morgan
words: Well, it got dominated by crooks and ambitious people that didn't want to work. Well, it just got rotten. They just quit it. Nobody would attend it. So it folded up. I attended lots of Chautauqua meetings around here, but Colton, Genesee, Clarkston, towns about that size.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did people get out of it when it was good?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Oh, when it first started it was pretty good. You saw these dinner clubs they used to have. Years gone by, you had dinner clubs. You had to join and pay so much a month to belong. You had got so many lectures, entertainment once a month and so on. They were the same way. I had four girlfriends by the name of Anderson that lived in Nez Perce. They were pretty good singers. They went two years on a tour in the West here. Revival meetings.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Revivals?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Well,

speaker: William Morgan
words: we have one now. Of course, Billy Grimm. Graham is one. Only he has got beyond the small. He is one of the big ones. But it is the same way over manager size. I heard Billy Sunda is around in those days. Yeah, Billy Sunda. Down in California used to have, what was that lady's name, the four square. The gospel of the four square.

speaker: William Morgan
words: I have heard of that.

speaker: William Morgan
words: She finally wound up in the scandal deal down on the beach.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was her name?

speaker: William Morgan
words: I went to see her show twice. Pretty good. Not a good show.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What were they like on the prairie?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Well, they would usually set up a tent. Once in a while they would have it in the building. And the preacher would come and he would usually have some kind of entertainment with him. Like a couple of singers. Men, women. Or maybe a couple more preachers. And they would preach not necessarily any creeds. They wouldn't preach creeds. They would preach religion as a whole. Whether you are a Catholic or a Protestant. Or whether you are a branch of the Catholic religion or a branch of the Protestant religion. They made no mention of that. It was just religion in general. And you could, most all the places, you could join the church of your choice.

speaker: William Morgan
words: His job was to preach the gospel and get you to join the church of your choice. So it was popular. Most of them talked religion as he claimed written in the Bible. I think it was entertaining. It was educational. Pretty good. Most of the old straight liners, howling metallists or the dirty shirt Baptists or whatever you want to call them. Why, they had their own ideas of their creed and they were like a preacher. They didn't talk their religion, their creed. You had to be sunk under. You had to be baptized under the water. And not sprinkled. And so on.

speaker: William Morgan
words: But these guys wouldn't talk that way mostly. No.

speaker: William Morgan
words: They were talking in general.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they produce conversions and that kind of thing?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Oh yes. Oh sure. People used to join the revival meetings by the hundreds. They'd go two or three times to church and maybe someone would stick. Yes.

speaker: William Morgan
words: That's one thing I've heard people say about it. There was an awful lot of interest at the time and then a lot of backsliding. Oh yeah.

speaker: William Morgan
words: Well, it was the same way in any religion. Only this was magnified. You joined the church of your choice. You might join an entirely different church than your folks did. A lot of them did too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know, in your talking about some of this work you were doing as a kid, I was thinking, did the adults, you know, the grown men, did they help you much?

speaker: William Morgan
words: I mean, to show you what to do and how to do it. Oh yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Are they very good at that?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Oh yes. Sure.

speaker: William Morgan
words: I worked in a field with three head horses on a plow. I'm about 11 years old. My dad was with me. He had six head horses on a two bottom gang and I had a one bottom. But I followed along behind what my old three horses do just as much about as I did or more. They just followed along behind. I'd guide them of course. Now and then at the corners I'd help on the harnessing, harness them and feed them. I usually fed them while Dad unharnessed them. But I worked in the field right alongside of him. He'd show me how to hitch up the high -haul. Well, when I was 16, I was a foreman of my neighbor's outfit. He had a thrash mount. I was just a kid. I'd show all the men what to do. What horses to work, where they belong, what size colors they wore. Sure, you bet you. The older people taught all the kids.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did you get that much responsibility?

speaker: William Morgan
words: You knew more about it than any other men. Well, I was raised right there and he knew or my neighbors knew what I could do. I worked in the field since I was 11 years old.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You need help with some hands?

speaker: William Morgan
words: No. I need some help to get a hand. Yeah. There's some right there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What? Nobody sitting here?

speaker: William Morgan
words: No. Sandy, man. No, this man here will help you in just a minute.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do they get through?

speaker: William Morgan
words: No, they...

speaker: William Morgan
words: You got help, sure. The only time I ever got fired, and it only lasted about an hour, I wanted to learn to milk a cow. The fellow I worked for took me out to the dairy barn, which he had, and showed me how to milk. So every night, instead of going in and eating dinner and going to bed like the men did, I stayed with him and helped him milk. Well, one night, or it might have been morning, one of the men had used a cow barn for an outhouse. And he stepped in there. Made him mad, he fired me. He thought it was me. And I wouldn't... I quit right then and there. I wouldn't have nothing to do with him. So he came back, thought it over, and gave me 50 cents a day increase in wages if I'd stay. And the next year, I became his foreman.

speaker: William Morgan
words: But then I could handle horses. As I told you, I broke 11 head one summer, and I was just a kid. And I could handle horses. I knew how deep to plow. I knew what was wrong with the binder. I could tie the knots on the twine that bound the bundles. I could fix that, put the chains on. Just like any man could. In fact, better than most of them. But I was raised. All right. What happened is you couldn't take a team of horses out there with the wrong size collars on. They'd come in at noon, all skinned up here. Boy, Dad would use a board on me short. Of course, I knew better than that.

speaker: William Morgan
words: No, you're teaching. Much of your teaching was done at home. I just found the practical teaching.

speaker: William Morgan
words: You didn't learn it in school.

speaker: William Morgan
words: You learned ABCs and how to read, write, and spell. When I was in sixth grade, I graded you all by numbers. 100%, 95%, 90. I got 28%. I couldn't spell cap. I wasn't interested in learning how to spell cap. I was interested in going with my dad when he had to carload the hogs.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: We had to what?

speaker: William Morgan
words: He was a stock buyer. We'd buy stock hogs and cattle. Carload at a time, shipping out a Kamiah or Peck or Culdesac up here. And shipping to the coast and butchers, slaughterhouses. At the World's Fair in 1908, I went from Kamiah, Idaho to Seattle as a caretaker of two -car load of cattle. My dad was with me too. He had two cars too. The trailer was allowed to one man for every two -car load. You had to unload those cattle every so many hours.

speaker: Unknown Speaker
words: Feed them water?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Yeah.

speaker: Unknown Speaker
words: They were bound for the fair?

speaker: William Morgan
words: No, bound for Seattle. A bunch of shops. Slaughterhouse. That was in 1908. It was the Seattle Yukon Exposition.

speaker: William Morgan
words: I had an advantage lots of times with the other kids. I've done a lot of things like that that other kids didn't get to do. They just sit in for it. I like to do things.

speaker: William Morgan
words: There's a cider mill I had made. I had five of them made. I got five more coming in ten. That's like the old mill. Well, when we were a year before I graduated out of high school, Dad used to peddle cider. We had two horses and what they called a hack. They'd take a 50 -gallon barrel of cider and go downtown. Peddle, two bits of gallow. My brother and me, it was our job, to crank that darn thing and press the juice out. It took about two days to make 50 gallons. Loads the barrel of cider. Pick the apples and wash them. Put them in the grinder and crush them all up and crush them out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Richard, do you have any such animal?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Us kids used to do all kinds of things like that and that's what made the difference. These poor kids now, I feel sorry for them. Most of them don't know a single number. Wear long hair and let their beard go out of the water.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Does that mean that you pretty much worked most of the day when you were not at school?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Sure you did. You betcha. When you got out of school, you fed the hogs, got the cattle, milk cows, helped mill, took care of the horses, fed them, cleaned out the barn. Huh. Get on the ranch and find out. Well, I've done a little of it. Nothing like that. Nothing like every day. It means every day too. Sundays are gone. If the horse eats on Sundays, so does the cows.

speaker: William Morgan
words: We each had a saddle horse. I had a pony. I rode the saddle right after the cattle. Of course, lots of times, much of the time, the cattle would come in to eat.

speaker: William Morgan
words: So we wouldn't maybe have to go very far. Maybe we wouldn't have to go at all.

speaker: William Morgan
words: I know this sounds, you know, you're talking about how nowadays the way kids are about work, and I agree, it sure has changed an awful lot.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But when you've been in, did you get a, did you find it fun? Did you get a joy out of doing that work?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Or was it just a... Pretty much so. Pride. Let's put it that way. Pride.

speaker: William Morgan
words: He was proud to be a man. Proud. There's just as much work as a hired man over at John Jones. I think that was the general consensus. The law people.

speaker: William Morgan
words: I played the fiddle a little bit of now. One of the fellows in our group, the name is Jeffries over here in Clarkson. He was very active in this literary society, him and his father. And his father was very poor, but they did make a little money. And he used to take a team in one of these hacks, and during the grain hauling in the fall, he'd travel down to the elevator and haul watermelons and cantaloupe and roasters and all that stuff and peddle to the farming section. And they liked him because he was a little fellow. He is more than 5 foot. I don't think he was much over 5 foot 6 or 5 foot 5. But he is. This is all it is. And they'd buy melons off of him. Some would trade him wheat, some would trade him chickens. He would bring the chickens back over there and try to sell them back here. He would bring the trade, watermelon for the chickens, a couple of watermelons for the trade, anything. He could get it. But he didn't have it on. And that's the way he got his stuff. He gets quite a kick out of telling them about different things that happened, how some of the men would try to cheat. He would try to keep them to a certain extent. Everybody had some cows, hogs.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that the main interest?

speaker: William Morgan
words: It was about even. We had more stock than any of the rest of them, yes. Because my dad was a stock buyer. If he saw a chance to buy a bunch of stock, cattle or steers, or cows, well he'd buy them. He had a very unusual set up with the bank. He was a good friend of the bankers. He can't do that nowadays. He didn't have any money. They'd always wait for old Dan Martin. But he'd pay you cash. He'd find a bunch of hogs, got a good buy on them, got a check, got that on the bank. He never had a cent in there. Oh yeah, I don't sell them if I ever have a cent, but he didn't have enough to pay for the hogs. That check was a note. Cashable when the stock was sold.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: A lot of guys couldn't give you that, huh? They had to buy it on time or something?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Well, the Irish fellow was not versed in stock like my dad was. He was honest. He'd buy this stock, take it to a railroad, ship it, get over to the auction house or some packing house, get his draft. He'd never accept the draft as mail to the bank to cover all these checks he'd read. That was banking in those days. Since the federal government owns our banks, runs our banks, you can't do those things.

speaker: William Morgan
words: Well, the big one must have been completely trusted in by the president of the bank. That's right.

speaker: William Morgan
words: Couldn't be other ones. Of course, a man couldn't commit much of a crime either. He couldn't get away, he couldn't get out of town in an automobile.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where did he get his stock from, usually? Did he go all around the country to buy it?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Neighborhoods, neighbors. Most of it was local. Yeah, well you couldn't go very far in a day, 20 miles of horseback.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So how would he do that? Would you buy it at a certain age usually and then raise them up on your land?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Well, that depends entirely.

speaker: William Morgan
words: We did just about everything. We raised some of them. I know at one time I remember that Dad took two four horse teams and two hog wagons and traveled about 25 miles over towards the town of Cottonwood. And he drove two nights and two days, you know, wearing a bag with these loads of pigs. Brought them home, we raised them, fattened them and drove them to Marcus. I remember that very plainly because I got up at night and here Dad comes with his four horse load of hogs and another man. He had a load two and I moved them. Nice, but very far.

speaker: William Morgan
words: So there's a lot of variation between horse and wagons.

speaker: William Morgan
words: Generally speaking, we tried to raise them as we could because we didn't cost anything to raise them. When Dad bought them, all them old sows were smarter than we were. They could tell where we planted our potatoes on the far side of our ranch. They could go and find them anytime. It was an old sow with pigs smarter than you think. She could sense the potato patch anywhere.

speaker: William Morgan
words: That's a fact. You may think that's kind of funny because it isn't. We got a good little snout on. A doggone pig, you know. There's a picture on the wall over there of Cottonwood Audio where a fellow trained for a hogs. He pulled a wagon and he took them to the World's Fair in San Francisco in 1982. 1915. 1915.

speaker: William Morgan
words: I'm taking pigs. I understand. It's rated as number one. It's the smartest time of the year. It's either a pig or a horse.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What could you do with most of everything you fed them? That we fed them?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Oh yeah. And the other stock too. You didn't have to buy grain, buy hay, any of that stuff. Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How many acres were you?

speaker: William Morgan
words: They had farming. 160. Well they all had 160. They had no money to buy and they bought this place. I wonder how much stock you could turn over in a year. Well not very much. Usually just off hand there's kind of a wide guess about 20 head hogs you can raise. Very comfortable. And usually cattle you'd probably have three or four cows. Maybe it ended in this because you always won fresh milk.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you think put him in such good state?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Say he's at the bank or the house. Fine, thank you very much. About his business sense. Oh, I suppose. I suppose. Yeah, anytime you do that. I don't know what. I couldn't say what the banker thought of him. I don't know. But of course he was in the different environments that you got now. Now you're living in a bunch of, let's just say college graduates who are striving for money. Get it honestly. Get it, but get it. Those days you didn't do that. You couldn't get away. You didn't have any politics to get your job either. You had to live a pretty straight life. There was no other way about it. When my dad was raised, he was a Welshman raised in Wales. He graduated from college. Wrestling, fighting, singing. That constitutes his graduation in those days.

speaker: William Morgan
words: In Wales. That is a college graduation. That's what he had three things he had to know.

speaker: William Morgan
words: My dad never was whipped but once. I hit him over the head with a neck yoke or a wagon.

speaker: William Morgan
words: He'd get on a spree once in a while. But never so he couldn't handle himself. He was an athlete, very, very strong. And he was a good singer. When we lived at the France Hotel down here, they used to buy him all his liquor. Get him to sing. He was a good singer.

speaker: William Morgan
words: He's a good pool player. He played cards. Pretty good card player. He took care of himself. He was himself first. But he was still a good mixer with everybody. He never got into the gambling stage, if that's what you call it.

speaker: William Morgan
words: He probably could have, but he didn't. He stuck to the farm. Buying and selling stock. And it's just one of these things. You can't have that now. Your stock sales are out here at the stockyards. Auctions. Your buyers are not buying 10 head of pigs or 10 head of... ...shokes or whatever it might be. Two or three cows or three or four steers. You don't buy them anymore. You go out here and you raise three or four hundred head of cattle out here in the cattle ranches. And you sell maybe forty or fifty at a time. It's a different setup.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about your mother? What was she like?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Your mother.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was she like?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Oh! She was a very good looking woman. She was five foot eight. Tall as that tallest gal in there. Let's see one here. And she was a dressmaker.

speaker: William Morgan
words: And... ...made hats.

speaker: William Morgan
words: And always well dressed. Of course, she probably made the same dress out of different... I mean made different dresses out of the same cloth.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did she sell them for money too?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Oh yeah.

speaker: William Morgan
words: Lots of people had her make their dresses. Like a wedding dress or a nice dress to wear and so on. And when you bought your clothes those days, that is the way my dad did.

speaker: William Morgan
words: He'd get whatever money he could and... ...we'd send us here and what about... ...we'd get the year's supply of everything. In other words, we'd get a harness and a collar for a couple of horses. And a pair of shoes for each one of us kids. And maybe a suit of clothes for one or two of us. Mother would get cloth goods to make into clothes. Underwear.

speaker: William Morgan
words: Christmas candy, nuts, sugar, syrup, coffee and all that stuff. We'd take a four horse team and take a load of wheat from the farm to market and sell it. Bring back this load of stuff from the depot.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What made it better to go to Sears? What made it better to go to Sears?

speaker: William Morgan
words: To buy exactly what you wanted in it. Oh, I don't know. Well, in the first place most of the stores didn't have it. In other words, they didn't have yard goods so much. But, that's the way Dad done it. I don't know whether he done any better or not. I couldn't tell you. Well, how common do you think it was for people to...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: ...do you think most people were cat mail order?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Some of them did. The better ones that had better clothes and so on, usually did. Only the poor people had to charge. Of course, those days you'd charge a lot of stuff. You didn't pay cash. It was generally to run a grocery bill by the year. In other words, let's run the grocery bill when and after you sold your grain.

speaker: William Morgan
words: And you start charging for nothing here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Could people in the prairie run a grocery bill down in Bozeman here?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Some of them did, yes. It didn't have to be just a little pack or something.

speaker: William Morgan
words: I knew one family traded the grocery store out here on 21st street for about 12 years. And they only paid their bill once a year. Whenever they sold their grain.

speaker: William Morgan
words: They did hard enough groceries, I would think.

speaker: William Morgan
words: Well, they bought that way too.

speaker: William Morgan
words: Although, they usually put up some security, either their ranch or stock or something. Or maybe the local bank put it up. Those days, the money was run by money lenders. Ranchers here in Lewiston used to be the big warehouse over here at Lewiston Mercantile Company. And they would sell a store, their supplies, payable whenever they got any money. And from one month to the next, maybe it would be a year. Maybe they owed three or four thousand dollars in notes, which would be fifteen, twenty thousand dollars.

speaker: William Morgan
words: But people were honest those days, generally. Not like this bunch of skin quits we got now. Hell, right today you can't transfer one man out of ten.

speaker: William Morgan
words: See, your morale of your country is so low today that you don't trust anybody. And your laws are governed by your law graduates, law school. And there's not much of a law anymore. Nobody honors the law. It's all judge. The judge, however the judge rules.

speaker: William Morgan
words: And that judge is political. Democrat or Republican.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And you've got into the same old, we don't have liberty or justice anymore, huh?

speaker: William Morgan
words: None. You don't have such a thing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You feel that there really used to be? You think there really used to be in the early days?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Well, yes. You're right. A man was as good as his word. That was the old slogan for years and years and years.

speaker: William Morgan
words: Do you know Bill Jones?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Yeah. Yes, sir. His word is as good as his name. It's the old saying.

speaker: Unknown Speaker
words: Are we interested in coming to you? You say, do you know Smith?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Yeah, say he's an honest man too. If you sell him anything, you get your money too. Not anymore. Sell him anything if you get the money if you can catch him.

speaker: William Morgan
words: We've got far too much education. College -wise, book -wise. It isn't worth that. You get out here, the average college graduate comes in here and applied for a job, and pays attention to you. Because he doesn't know anything. All he knows is the books and puts all the books you want across the front of that building, you don't make this place. I'll take those two or three men out there and you can have all the books you want and put them up there. That's the truth. That's why your failures of all these big businesses, there'll be our grant. 250 of the biggest stores in the United States.

speaker: William Morgan
words: Newberry, Woolworths, Yellow Front. Look at them. Roscoe Temple.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Roscoe Temple. Big outfit. They have big warehouses and apartments. They're all having plenty of trouble. That's why they have the stock market. They keep building new ones.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why?

speaker: William Morgan
words: To sell stock. The stock ain't worth anything when it comes out to one that's all leveled off. It's all done by executives, stockholders, auditors.

speaker: William Morgan
words: I figure these guys are in trouble in these companies because they're all experts in books.

speaker: William Morgan
words: Books.

speaker: William Morgan
words: The average person may not believe it, but there it is right out there in the street.

speaker: William Morgan
words: Oh, you can see it. I certainly agree with you.

speaker: William Morgan
words: Hell, we're building a piece downtown here. We're building this Morgan's Alley. Well, I own two places down there. Between Third and Fourth, facing Main and facing D. At one time, that was the property. Our welfare and our police department and our fire department. And then our commissions, zoning commissions. And our county and city both, zoning commissions. And all this environmental stuff, ecology, the whole business of it has just took business and just crunched it. In favor of labor to be elected.

speaker: William Morgan
words: And health and welfare. Something for nothing. I draw Social Security myself. I don't get a salary. But the poor bastard that's working for me out here is paying it. You're paying it too. I'm 81 years old and you've got the rest of your life to pay what I spent. Figured any way you wanted, that's the way it is.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you say a man's word was as good as his note, and he was known in the early days in a way, if he'd done something wrong, something shady, would the word get around fast?

speaker: William Morgan
words: You bet you.

speaker: William Morgan
words: You bet you did. Yes, sir. His neighbors might be the very ones that turned him in and the ones that may come after him too. Yes, sir.

speaker: William Morgan
words: That's the truth. Today, you do something wrong, oh well. He's a good Democrat. He didn't mean to. And that's the truth. That's the way it is. Christ, I can sit here with you all and tell you people, I've been here a long time, know lots of people. Naturally, being in this business here all 55 years right here, in this building. Seven years, house to house. That little picture of that little wagon up there, the horse, going from one house to another. Some coffee, tea, soap.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Will you tell me how you first got into that? How you first started?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Well, yeah, I worked. I told you I was forming this Threissen outfit. I made and saved $250. And one lump sum, that was what I was paid at the end of that deal. Well, when I got to grade home, I was tired, just worn completely out, so I came, my folks were living in Clarkson. So I came down to my mother's place, stayed with her, and tried to find help, work, tried to find work. I couldn't buy a job. I went over to this big building, second house from here, and offered to work at the big Mark and Dale house for three months for nothing. No pay, just to get experience. They wouldn't even give me a job for nothing.

speaker: William Morgan
words: No pay. Well, I went downtown.

speaker: William Morgan
words: I gave the cashier at the bank two bits if he'd give me any information to get a job. The fella thought I was crazy. I'll never forget that. Well, I went on down home, and the fella moved in from Genesee up here next to me on one of them tea wagons. So I went over and talked to him, wondered what he'd done and how he'd done it and all this and that. Well, he says, next Monday I want to be on my route.

speaker: William Morgan
words: Why don't you come ride with me?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Alright, so next Monday came, and we started in down at where Lewis Tuck Hotel is, and we came up Bigger Street. It used to be C Street. The first place we stopped at, he says, well, here's a card. Now, this lady might be home. And if she's home, she may buy something from you. And I'll go across the street because there's a woman over here who wants a waffle iron. Those days you peddled anything. So I went over, went in behind the house.

speaker: William Morgan
words: This woman came to the door and said, hello, Willie, what are you doing here?

speaker: William Morgan
words: She ran a little red lighthouse down here on the street, and everything. She had a good night because she wanted what I was doing. She said, come on in. I didn't know. I knew her, yes, that's as far as I knew her.

speaker: William Morgan
words: So she said, what are you selling?

speaker: William Morgan
words: I said, coffee. Once she said, I'll buy some coffee. And she told me how much she wanted and some tea and some soap. I got all through. I read down this list that was on this card, and I thanked her and went on out to the car.

speaker: William Morgan
words: And this fellow said, well, did you get an order?

speaker: William Morgan
words: I said, yeah, yeah, she went quite loosely. He read this thing down.

speaker: William Morgan
words: He said, did you sell all that stuff?

speaker: William Morgan
words: I said, no, I said, she told me that's what she wanted. And I said, I know her folks and the family, and she'll pay. She's got money.

speaker: William Morgan
words: She said, how do you know?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Well, she knows me. I think I'll show you. Oh, no. It's all right. He sat there and figured a little bit.

speaker: William Morgan
words: He said, you know how much you made?

speaker: William Morgan
words: I want to interrupt you today, Bill Martin. I haven't seen you for 35, 40 years.

speaker: William Morgan
words: How are you, Bill?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Pretty good.

speaker: William Morgan
words: How are you?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Fine, thanks. You're good. You're going to have to work a little harder. You're going to have to work a little harder.

speaker: William Morgan
words: Yeah, you and I had better stand up side to side, didn't we?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Well, I don't see much difference. You haven't changed, Bill. Well, I can sit down easier than I used to. Yeah. Anyhow. I figured it out. He said, you made a little over two and a half. Just something hit me then. I worked for two and a half a day, and I was getting 50 cents more than the rest of them. Because I was kind of the foreman on the part. And I said, in 30 minutes I'll make two and a half. I said, I'm not going to look at those teddyhatter horses at the rear end anymore. I'm not going to look at them.

speaker: William Morgan
words: So, I started driving the coffee and tea wagon. I never did make two and a half in 30 minutes until the second year. The first year I was on the run, I was $30 a month gross. I had to feed my horse and live myself. $30 a month. The boss from Spokane that I was working with, he says, wrote me a letter. Then he called my mother. He says, well, if Will will stay another year, we'll teach him how to sell. I said, I think he can sell. So, she says, well, you come down, I'll talk to you. So, he came down on the train. He says, well, if you'll stay for a year, I'll teach you how to sell. So, he sent a man down. He was a professional tramp. That's all he ever done was tramp. He had a card in his pocket. A man by the name of Jones. He was president of the Grand Union Tea Company. He says, give this man a job anytime, anywhere he is in the United States.

speaker: William Morgan
words: So, he's the one that taught me how to sell, how to run away business. I started in. Then after that, in October, I started the first general. I got really out to business. I won five contests in the West of the United States selling drinks. I won one in Seattle, two in Spokane, one to sell in coconut chocolate, one to sell in brewing, one to sell in starch, one to sell in rice. In rice sales, I placed second in the nation.

speaker: William Morgan
words: Now, they paid my expenses.

speaker: Unknown Speaker
words: Do you tell this lady something about continental seasoning?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Well, they give you 30 days, a 30 -day selling period. They're all the way the same.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were you still operating out of a wagon?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Yeah, I'm taking a picture of you.

speaker: William Morgan
words: Well, I'm going to ask you a little more in detail here. This company, starting with this guy, you went with him the first time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did you go from there?

speaker: William Morgan
words: You had to get a wagon of your own. Well, no, he tried to fleece me out of $250. He found out it was $250, and so he offered me half interest in the job. I'm a dumb kid. I didn't know. So, I agreed to pay him $250 for half interest in his job. I said, I can make two and a half in 30 minutes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How'd you get along?

speaker: William Morgan
words: You looked like the first mentor. My mother wouldn't go for it, only under conditions. She had an attorney. The attorney lived next door to us. So, she went over and talked to us and said, let me fix it. So, he put the money in escrow over here in the Idaho First National Bank, providing, if I was accepted as their agent to drive this wagon, my red horse, he could have the money. But my mother saw to it that I wasn't accepted in the 30 days. So, she went and got the money out of the bank. And he skipped the country because the old guy graduated from TKM, made about $750, unbeknownst to me. That's how I got the job.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did she fix it that you didn't get a broom in a month?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Well, I don't know just what else she told them, but anyhow, I didn't get any appointments.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So, then you got it after he's been married?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Then you took over his round.

speaker: William Morgan
words: Well, I noticed that Tom Campbell says in that story that she had to push a little bit to get you selling in the first place.

speaker: William Morgan
words: He wrote that in there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He wrote that in there?

speaker: William Morgan
words: No, that's right. When I went out the first time on my own, that wagon my mother went with me. And I was active in the church. I went to Sunday school and went to the Denver meetings on Wednesdays.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Which church?

speaker: William Morgan
words: First Christian Church. Over in Clarkson. That's a big one. It used to be the biggest one there.

speaker: William Morgan
words: So, she went with me and the first sale we made, I was showing my stuff and she took over and she got the order. That was the first sale that I was credited with. That's what he wrote up there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This bill that skipped the country, did you go around with him for that period of time?

speaker: William Morgan
words: No, well, I went, yeah, just about a month.

speaker: William Morgan
words: That's how that's done. But anyhow, it's kind of funny. Then the boys came from Kansas and it was salted in salt brine, roasted in an oven at such and such a degree. So and so. So you got to know that's all. There's nothing else for the master seller. Person to person. A lot of people think you've got to be a come digger of a talker. No, I couldn't talk for myself either.

speaker: William Morgan
words: I was heading to the toastmaster's to help reorganize the toastmaster's. I can't talk today. I haven't a voice that's any good at all for conversation. I can't talk to a crowd and make them hear me because my voice is on the guttural side. I know what I'm talking about. I can gesture and do pretty good. But as far as a voice, like some of these fellows on radio, that takes time. That's hard to do.

speaker: William Morgan
words: You know, you call this guy a tramp.

speaker: William Morgan
words: When you say tramp, he was a tramp.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you mean by that?

speaker: William Morgan
words: He just never had a home or he never had anywhere to go. He just went live from daylight to dark and he had a hotel room. Always selling enough stuff to make enough money to go to the next town. He had no ambition.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he work for the Grand?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Grand Union Teacup.

speaker: William Morgan
words: He actually didn't work for him. He worked on commission for him. He got nothing unless he sold it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he drink him?

speaker: William Morgan
words: No, no, no. No smoking. A lot of tramps, you know. That was part of it. I had the idea. But for him it was just a kind of way of living.

speaker: William Morgan
words: Well, put it that way. They called him a tramp.

speaker: William Morgan
words: So do I. Well, I've heard of tramp trainers. Tramps in a lot of fields.

speaker: William Morgan
words: When I was in the Army band, a fellow played baritone. I played trombone and I got quite a while acquainted with him. He was a professional tramp. He worked with Barnum Bailey Circus. And he played in the band. Just as soon as their act was over, he went downtown. Went up all the alleys. Every alley in every town. Pick up all the brass, the copper, all the metals and stuff. He carried the gunny sacks. He'd take it down and ship it to his central home somewhere in Tennessee. And then in the fall when the circus is through, he'd go down there at that storage place that he shipped it to. Pick it up, clean it up. Self. That was his years wages.

speaker: William Morgan
words: He was the goddamn baritone player you ever saw. You get him out on the street and pretty soon, wham! You'd hear that old baritone singing all the way up there. He just blowed the bell off of him. In just a minute he'd run out of wind, he'd stop. He just couldn't go any farther. And then the band and some other fella would take over. Maybe a cornet or something. And then pretty soon you'd hear that old baritone again. He thought that she'd come and he just... And in the army band he played the same. Whenever he was playing the baritone, you could hear that old baritone singing above the whole bunch of them. Just started ripping it. And I'll never forget that. And he visited all the alleys. He'd tell me where the towns were and how far they were apart and the alleys. What he used to do. Now people chase him off once in a while.

speaker: William Morgan
words: Seems like a real hard way to live, you know. I mean to be on the bottom all the time. Yeah. Guess you see a lot of places.

speaker: William Morgan
words: He never stole anything. He said, I never did steal anything. If I found some stuff, I'd ask him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This tramp that taught you, did he teach you how to sell a lot instead of a little? I mean, how to get people to buy a lot?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Yes. Yes. He did. He'd build a sale up. He'd build it up. I'll never forget one thing. One day I was up here at Craigmont. In those days they used to have wagons out over the country selling Watkins products, Raleigh products, medicines, patent medicines. This fellow named Randolph. Oh yeah. So we ate breakfast at the Craigmont Hotel. He introduced himself and I did. He says, oh you're the son of a gun that steals my orders all the time. He says, I'd like to see you. I'd like to see you go with you sometime. I says, well I'm going out right away right with you now.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where are you going?

speaker: William Morgan
words: I told him not to. Some people made of Randall. R -A -M -D -A -N -O -L -A. Randall. So they leave out, oh I know which one you're going to. Russ. I said Russ Randall's place. Well I was just there last week. You can't sell nothing there. Oh well, I'll make a difference. I'll go out anyhow.

speaker: William Morgan
words: So we drove out there.

speaker: William Morgan
words: Both of them was home. Him and Herb bought. I'd sold them before. Two or three times. They bought usually six months of stuff at a time. Maybe a year. Said yeah we've been looking for you. We want a box of raisins and a case of salmon. And I don't remember what the rest of the stuff was.

speaker: William Morgan
words: Anyhow, she says well I can't buy any extracts or spices from you. Mr. So and So was just here last week and I bought from him. I said that's fine. I says you probably bought, he's just got small bottles. I says you'll have to buy before first year anyhow. Now I want to show you an orange I got. I said orange flavoring. So I got my sample out. I learned very well to pour it out and take the finger. It didn't cause a friction. It's an evaporation.

speaker: William Morgan
words: If you get it up close to your nose you've got the not too orange flavor. I learned that. Oh she says my God that's just delicious.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Does that extract?

speaker: William Morgan
words: I sure do. So one learner don't like it. Well maybe we'd better. I done sent me some pepper and some cinnamon and something else. That right. I'll take them.

speaker: Unknown Speaker
words: I says how big is it coming in?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Well I said you've been buying quarts of vanilla. I don't think you'd want a quart of this. Probably need a pint of lemon and a quart of vanilla and about 8 ounces of orange. I said that'd be alright. Send them all through. So I sent her, wrote down a quart of vanilla. Find the lemon and this 8 ounce bottle of orange. In other words this is my base to work from to get the bigger sizes out of there. It came to White Rose Soap. Now White Rose Soap was quite common for those days in truck stores. They couldn't put a hell of a price on it. We had a fairly good price. I think it was 85 cents a box. A dozen.

speaker: William Morgan
words: She asked me two or three questions about how they come. I told her. She said well I'll take a dozen. I said three dozen in a box. Well that's alright. She wrote down three dozen.

speaker: William Morgan
words: We got outside. I said I'll be god damn. She said you lazy bastard. You've never done one god damn thing. I went out in the car and got my little bottles. I brought them in here and put them away. I went out and got some more stuff and put them away. You sat there and done nothing. You sold four times more than I did. You've never done a darn thing.

speaker: William Morgan
words: I said what was I supposed to do?

speaker: William Morgan
words: The company ships it to them. They said yes there is nothing to do. White Rose Soap comes three dozen. You're no damn well out of here. I said listen just a minute now.

speaker: William Morgan
words: You sure it's not so?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Well no it's probably a pack of dozens. But you want to sell three dozen so you told her it would come three dozen in a box.

speaker: William Morgan
words: What difference does it make?

speaker: William Morgan
words: If you're going to get three dozen soap, I don't rather ship all three in one box or three dozen boxes. All right you're god damn. All the way back to town I said well I'll tell you I'll buy you a dinner. All right sir god damn you. I'll buy you dinner. All your white rose soap comes three dozen in a box. A dozen story. A dozen story. It reminds me of the way I got out one time up at the pier. I was selling it and a guy says, he showed me the items and how they packed. I said a dozen and he said well give me half a dozen. So I sent him a case. Next week when I got up there he says god damn it.

speaker: Unknown Speaker
words: He said how come you sent me a case of that stuff?

speaker: Unknown Speaker
words: I said I did and I thought you said I'll take a dozen. Let me have a dozen. I said let me have a dozen. He said no I said a half a dozen. This is my son. Good to meet you. I'm Sam Schrager. He's copied a lot of my stuff. He's got a lot of his own.

speaker: William Morgan
words: He's about as good as Sam is in this country out there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you teach him?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Oh no, he of course listened to me around here.

speaker: William Morgan
words: He's way out above any of you. You can't corner him. I don't care what you do. Nobody ever by likes him. He's got an enemy in this country.

speaker: Sam Schrager
