words: Well how long did it take you to build up your understanding of how to go about this?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Just one year.

speaker: William Morgan
words: I told you a while ago that I worked thirty dollars a month for the first year. The second year this trip came down. I was considered more than 250 a month. I was the highest paid grocery in this valley.

speaker: William Morgan
words: 250 a month.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And was it all door to door the way you done it?

speaker: William Morgan
words: That's right.

speaker: William Morgan
words: A lot of women. I'd go in and knock at the back door. She says well I've been expecting you.

speaker: William Morgan
words: She says what time is it?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Ten o 'clock.

speaker: William Morgan
words: Well that's my regular time isn't it?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Yeah, you said you'd be at ten. That is one little thing that I used to use. I'll be at ten o 'clock. Ten o 'clock I was there. I didn't give a damn if I killed a man on the way. I was there at ten o 'clock. And I used that to sell goods. Never late. Never early.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How often would you get to a place? Oh, well like that?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Well I would see to it I didn't make that statement too often. Maybe I'd pick one place that is hard to get to or easy to get to. And drive back. Go out along the way just to make that one statement. But it paid off. That tea man, he says he'd be here at ten o 'clock. He'd be here at ten o 'clock every time. That's why I bought him a tea for him.

speaker: William Morgan
words: Well that sounds silly. I know. It isn't. It's salesmanship. It's something that's the average person. More than a monkey would have. It's silly. Well maybe it is. As long as it makes the sales.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It was probably mostly women that you would sell to, right?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Yeah. Well those days were there. Then after I took the job out of Seattle, men would come in. Because they spend more money. A woman won't spend much money. Only on herself.

speaker: William Morgan
words: But I dealt with men, men. Not the thrashing crews. And then I worked for a commercial company coming out of Seattle. And I'd take orders and ship. That's why this rando I was talking about. I shipped there every day.

speaker: William Morgan
words: In other words, when at first you were selling right off the wagon, you were selling to the other trucks.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that when you started making big money?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Gosh.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that when you started making bigger money when you moved into the shipping stuff?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Yeah.

speaker: William Morgan
words: Oh yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So that meant you could carry a lot more stuff in the wagon?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Well I didn't carry anything in the wagon. Oh. I just took orders. I just had an order book. And a sample case. Sample, that's what I did.

speaker: William Morgan
words: Oh, you couldn't carry that stuff. He never did make any money. He just acquired more stuff.

speaker: William Morgan
words: Well that's one of the monsters here. Of course, another thing in selling. Old daddy told me he never made any money until the last one sold. And there ain't no way he can get rid of all this shit.

speaker: William Morgan
words: Well, selling game is the highest paid profession on earth. There's no game, there's nothing, no profession of any kind that will equal selling. Doctor, lawyer, you got to sell yourself. I don't give a darn what you're doing or where you're at. You got to sell them. Doesn't make any difference. Anybody's profession, if you can't sell it, regardless of how good you are, isn't a success at all. You can see that every day. If you can sell something, you're on top. These politicians, nothing but salesmanship.

speaker: William Morgan
words: Most of them ain't worth a taker's damn for nothing. Crooked, live, everything else. They sell it to the public. You'd get money for it too. A gallon of pork.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you were selling in a wagon like that, when you were taking the stuff around for work, what would you sell mostly? Was it coffee, meringue, meat, beef, and rice?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Well, that's the main two things, yes. Of course, we had starch, as I told you before, and we had cocoa, and we had rice,

speaker: William Morgan
words: and bluing, and all those things were tops. My prices was always higher than anybody else's. Always. Always.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So it was quality?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Yeah, well, maybe not. But it was better than this common run. Let's put it that way. I didn't give a damn what the other fella had. I had a way to get around it.

speaker: William Morgan
words: I'd figure from the top, a quart bottle instead of a two ounce bottle, and so on down the line. And then I'd work back up. I'd get an order made of a two ounce bottle and work up to the bigger one.

speaker: William Morgan
words: Those days, of course, you could peddle off the wagon. I didn't do too much peddling. I would make the sale then and then take another order for the next delivery. I'd make two sales at a time, one for now and one for two weeks.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Selling the things that you didn't have on the wagon that time?

speaker: William Morgan
words: I'd sell things I had on the wagon, and I'd take it for what I didn't have for the next one.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Then when I got there next time, I'd deliver what was ordered plus what more they wanted.

speaker: William Morgan
words: Lots of time, maybe she had company, the woman, she'd get one other extra five bars of soap. I know where it is. Or most anything, she forgot to order.

speaker: William Morgan
words: So you'd always take the delivery order and take another one for the next time. See, I did that in the store when I had my store down in town. One of us was out selling all of them. We were in the store. We were out working the house down, selling anything they wanted. Lettuce, brown eggs, brown eggs. I'll never forget that. Some of these women, they liked brown eggs. It had to be brown shelled. No, sir. They tasted better and they were better in every way. So I'd get my cases of eggs in the store and sort them all out, any brown ones, I'd send them out to one side. Get a dime extra, a nickel extra, a dozen.

speaker: William Morgan
words: I didn't want them brown eggs, so they got them.

speaker: William Morgan
words: Same damn day, he says, well, it wasn't a theater probably. It was some different kind of a chicken. It had to be. I'll never forget that.

speaker: William Morgan
words: One of the fellows that worked with me turned out to be a preacher in the first Christian church. And he was in charge of national finance. The first Christian church. His office was in Kansas City, Orville Peterson. And he just passed away about two months ago. He worked for me from the time my brother left for college until he graduated at Oregon.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Eugene, which college is it?

speaker: William Morgan
words: University of Oregon. Yeah, University of Oregon. He graduated there.

speaker: William Morgan
words: He's a fine fellow.

speaker: William Morgan
words: Just about as nice a man as you ever saw. He worked for me for about four years. And when he graduated from college, he got a pulpit. A pulpit of his own. In the meantime, he worked for me during the summertime. Holidays, he got the chance to come back here.

speaker: William Morgan
words: I raised a kitty my lot. We sold malt and hops during the prohibition days. You get in a 50 -gallon barrel of hop and malt. And a bale of Yakima hops. I used to make all kinds of money on that stuff. These people come in and buy quarts and gallons. Five -gallon cans. I'd dish it out of these barrels.

speaker: William Morgan
words: He had a German people up here who lived up here on 7th Avenue. Used to run this big iron once just right up the street here. They owned it then. Nix Welling went up here. She made her own beer. Used lots of it. I'd usually try to fix it some way so he'd have to deliver it. With a malt valve. I'm not kidding about peddling beer.

speaker: William Morgan
words: He'd take it all the way to the salt. Dang you Bill, this is your turn to do it.

speaker: William Morgan
words: You used to tell them, the college used to tell them, before you worked and what all they'd done. Some of the girls there in the office. One day they got a package. We mailed it up here and we had a, we still do, so -called Sudsamore. Five -pound boxes. So he ordered a dozen bars, a pound bars of soap. And it's Sudsamore. So he'd send up here and have me send him the soap. This time we went to get them and somebody has moved them over to the main office. And I don't know why they done that. This girl said they were leaking.

speaker: William Morgan
words: We got the kid leaving and he went over there. And some way, I don't know how or just what all happened, but anyhow they sent the kid over by the leaking books.

speaker: William Morgan
words: They wanted to open it up and see if it was.

speaker: William Morgan
words: So he started to open it but something happened and he didn't. He got home and I'd send him a quart of malt and a package of hops. And I waited a couple of days and sent the soap. It was easy to pay for the soap. I don't know, I sat on the head. He says, God damn you, Billy. He says, if I ever opened up an area that would upset that college.

speaker: William Morgan
words: He got it. He wrote me a letter and I was over at my house. I'd show you a letter one time. He wrote, Kansas City. He still remembered to get in the hops and malts.

speaker: William Morgan
words: All this business isn't always somber.

speaker: William Morgan
words: Well, for selling around.

speaker: William Morgan
words: He did the same thing in Clarkson. He worked here, I told you, a month. He was here a month. He had every route, this street on a Monday. This street on Tuesday. This street on Wednesday. This one Thursday so he gets the missus. And this one here on Friday. And on Friday I pick up the missus on these two other routes. In other words, he had three routes to start off with. Monday, Tuesday, Wednesday, Thursday, Friday. But he put them in between so that if I miss Mrs. Joe Brown on Monday, I'm driving a horse. You can't like it on a mobile. You went three blocks over the next Tuesday, next day. Because her business might be valuable enough to go over and pick this up and then come back.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Does that mean then that you could do the whole town once a week?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Yeah. Well, it was every two weeks. Every two weeks. Next week was Clarkson.

speaker: William Morgan
words: Annasotan.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about the outlying areas?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Well, I never took that on until we went to the Seattle outfit. See, this was all local. You couldn't do anything with one horse and a wagon, you know. Not over these country roads.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was your percentage commission on this stuff?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Oh, our highest commission was 25%. Most of it was 20. But soap and washing powder and starch was only 10. I sell $100 of stuff to make $10. And that ain't very much. On a 30 cent box of starch, you made three pennies.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Rich?

speaker: William Morgan
words: I made 30 pennies.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did brand names mean very much to people at that time?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Well, you know, the way at least I heard it was in the early days, early, early days, most of them weren't even sold brand names. They kind of came in in the late 1800s to get real important. You know, that's what I heard. They got more and more important as time went on. Well, that's true. Somebody wanted one brand instead of another. Well, to show you what I mean now by that, Arm & Hammer Soda.

speaker: William Morgan
words: When I was selling, Arm & Hammer Soda was a soda. The other brand was Cow brand. It was two brands owned nicely. So Arm & Hammer bought up Cow brand and they turned it around and one side of the box was Cow brand, the other one was Arm & Hammer. So you put both brands on the shelf, Cow brand and Arm & Hammer.

speaker: William Morgan
words: Yeah, that's right. And a lot of other things are done the same way.

speaker: William Morgan
words: What am I?

speaker: William Morgan
words: I have a picture of a chicken. It hasn't scratched yet. It's scratching so properly. It's cleansing properly. And the little chicken sat there just out of the egg. It says here. I don't see that anymore. Well, all those things had its origin long about that time. Just before that, there was no Kellogg's Corn Flakes. It was forced, F -O -R -C -E, forced at a man.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Muscles?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Yeah. Shaped in Corn Flakes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Like Wheaties, huh?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Yeah. Take it off the same, just about the same thing. And about the same dialogue.

speaker: William Morgan
words: So a lot of the things that came on the market those days. Ivory soap, it floats. I don't know what difference it makes whether it floats or sinks. But that don't make a difference, it floats.

speaker: William Morgan
words: That was before they had the 99 and 44 100 percent. That came later.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Pure what?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Yeah. All those catchy things. Well, lots of people made fortunes over labeling. That finally disappeared with the chain stores. Safeway don't have anything. They have their own brands. You just buy Safeway.

speaker: William Morgan
words: It's a little cheaper. Supposedly. Than to buy their other brands on the shelf. Yeah.

speaker: William Morgan
words: Well, of course they don't have a house anymore. All is their own brand. Same with Albertson. Well, not Albertson, but URM and Spokane. They got a brand called Western Family. All it is is seconds. Now, seconds don't mean it's second grade to a certain extent. Seconds means that the top grade is sold under other labels. Then the next grade, which is the common grade, Garden Run or whatever you call it. Good stuff, but nothing fancy. That's Western Family. They don't sell anything first grade at all. And they are cheaper.

speaker: William Morgan
words: But you're also buying cheaper merchandise.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Anything else?

speaker: William Morgan
words: You don't have any such things. Like the women that you spoke to.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they depend on you for information a lot?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Yeah. A lot of times, yes. No, I don't have an holiday. Yes, sir.

speaker: William Morgan
words: I usually say, well, call up Mrs. Brown. You know Mrs.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Brown down here on the third?

speaker: William Morgan
words: I'd be awful careful on that though. She might be a different church member.

speaker: William Morgan
words: I know their religion too.

speaker: William Morgan
words: I got it right. Kind of touchy. If they were a Catholic, I'd better find it out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Find another Catholic for them to talk to you?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Of course, I sold them all. I get into Catholic settlement. I'd name all the Catholics that I knew. I'd better mention the others. And so on down the line. I got down one time after World War I. I was overseas in the army and I come back and I learned quite a little German and quite a little French. I was with an interpreter all the time. And I got down here to La Crosse in the Anticon. A Russian -German settlement. I sold a few bills of groceries. Speaking German. Just conversation German or phrasing German.

speaker: William Morgan
words: Dumb pluck or something like that. Dumb cup.

speaker: William Morgan
words: Placid loss, placid loose. Something like that you know. Heinz Weigand, FIM sex even and so on. I could talk that way. But I couldn't carry on a conversation. They'd call from one place to another. Say this man's selling coffee, he's alright. He'd talk German. Yeah, I got orders that way too.

speaker: William Morgan
words: Sounds funny but that's the way people are. Makes sense to me.

speaker: William Morgan
words: I get down there and I'd say, maybe I'd speak French, Quelle Elle Callee.

speaker: William Morgan
words: What time is it?

speaker: William Morgan
words: And then turn around and say, oh, das ist Franzse. I'm speaking German. And they get quite a kick out of it.

speaker: William Morgan
words: And all those things counts. It's just something that's, well you don't learn in school. You just don't.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about the school?

speaker: William Morgan
words: The suspiciousness that people might have for strangers. Like I would think some women would just be suspicious when a guy came out the door.

speaker: William Morgan
words: Well, that is true and they were too. Good Bill of Goods I sold up here at a little town Gifford. Gifford, Idaho. Maybe you know the people, I don't know. Summers,

speaker: William Morgan
words: S -U -M -E -R, Summers. Those two brothers, Lee and Charlie. Well, Charlie Summers and his wife always bought an order every year from me. A little groceries, like 50 pounds of peanut butter. And what not.

speaker: William Morgan
words: Well, Lee Summers was his brother. And they were just as opposite as two ends of a stick. He made the threats that a peddler ever came on his place. He'd run him off of the pitchfork. So I came and sold Charlie his scooters. Well, I said, you have a brother, Lee.

speaker: William Morgan
words: Oh, you don't see him?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Oh, no, he's deaf on peddlers.

speaker: William Morgan
words: What do you mean?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Oh, no, don't go over there. You'll get in trouble. He'd run a man off last week or last month. Well, no, he's a day to go. I just made up my mind, the hell I won't. He lived down the road about, oh, I guess a mile and a half across to the opposite. I went right down there. And he was quite ill. He went up to his house and settled on the flat up there. And it was just about noon. Just about dinner time. I drove up there and he was fixing the tire on his car.

speaker: William Morgan
words: I said, what's the matter, a little hard luck?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Oh, yeah, he can't get this tire fixed.

speaker: William Morgan
words: I said, let me see it, what's the matter?

speaker: William Morgan
words: I says, I got some good tire equipment, I can fix it. And I said, let's see it.

speaker: William Morgan
words: I says, did you test this? Do you know where the leak is?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Well, I think it's there.

speaker: William Morgan
words: You got a tub?

speaker: William Morgan
words: I want it filled by that full of water. Let's find out where the leaks are. I got a good pump out here, we'll find out where these leaks are. I said, get me some water in the tub, I'll fix your tire. I didn't tell him who I was or nothing. So he says, well, if you think you can fix it, all right. So we went and got a tub and we filled it full of water and I put that pump up. I said, what I told you, there's the leak over here.

speaker: William Morgan
words: See it?

speaker: William Morgan
words: I used the pump, see. I took it and marked it. I said, there's your leak. I said, you got one here too, but you fixed that. So I took it out and wiped it. And I had, those days we used a flat piece of iron, and we'd put it in there that way so you'd put gasoline in it and light it. You'd heat that hot and put that sticker on well and cement it on there with cement. So I fixed that patch and I went back to where he was working. I said, you didn't do a very good job with that. I said, I better do that over. So I done it over. I said, no, I think that's cool enough. I said, let's see if it leaks. So I pumped it up and the whole lot of spot. I said, now let's look in your tire.

speaker: William Morgan
words: What caused the leak?

speaker: William Morgan
words: I worked around in there and sure enough there had to have been a nail in there, but it had been pulled out. I said, now I got some glue and I'll put it on top of it and poke it in that hole the best I can. I says, first time you're in town you better get this welded, this hole welded up. This patch will probably last you, but it might not. So we put the tire on his car, took the jacket off. Well, he says, God, I'm glad you come along.

speaker: William Morgan
words: He says, you got an extra?

speaker: William Morgan
words: It's flat too. I said, get it out here, let's fix it up. So he said, oh my gosh, I'll just take you up on that. So he got this extra tire and got it out there and I run it through the water tub process and fixed it all up.

speaker: William Morgan
words: He says, who are you?

speaker: William Morgan
words: I said, I'm just a damn coffee peddler. I thought I'd stop by here and I'd like to eat dinner with you. I'll pay you for your dinner. I said, that's one thing I always did. I never bummed a meal in my life. I says, oh, I want to pay you for it. I understand that. I ain't sponging on nobody. Well, you're certainly welcome. Welcome for fixing that tire. That has nothing to do with it. This is different. This is my meal. So I ate dinner. During the conversation, he wasn't so bad. Of course, I knocked him clear down on the floor. Fixing that tire. He said he had no use for peddlers like that. I don't blame you. I don't either. You got them peddlers, you can't trust any of them. But I said, as far as I'm concerned, I don't consider myself a peddler because I don't peddle anything off my car. I ship it to you. I says, Lee, I call him B's first name. I said, Lee, I said, I checked you at the bank. Now, be it different. You got a good reputation. So I'll tell you what I'll do. Charlie has bought from us. Your brother. Yeah, I know he buys them all the peddlers everywhere. He's smart enough to know what he's doing. I'll ship you this stuff and you send me a check. It's like I tell you if it isn't, here's my phone number and here's where I live in Clarkson. You call me up and I'll come and get it. You can ask me. Well, by God, you seem to be a pretty fair sort of a fella. I'll just try you out once. I've never done this before. So he gave me a nice little order, it was in the big one. All these friendly views.

speaker: William Morgan
words: I shipped it to him. He kept taking me for the tire. I said, no, you don't take much of that. I'll do that for anybody. I said, I hate to see a man's... ...car that can't run it.

speaker: William Morgan
words: You know he bought from them from then on?

speaker: William Morgan
words: I was the only one he ever bought from. When actually it was a goddamn tire deal, he put it over.

speaker: William Morgan
words: I can see myself fixing that damn tub in that water. But if I hadn't got that tub out there, I'd have probably not... ...

speaker: William Morgan
words: ...a lot of funny experiences. I could send it all afternoon. One of the best experiences I ever had was down here at Pummore. The name was Smith. He was on a big farm, he had about 1 ,200 acres. Those were the days that you had to permit to buy liquor. And he drank lots of whiskey. All his crew did.

speaker: William Morgan
words: So, it was in the afternoon... ...and he'd build a gate. All these fancy gates that you had to pull things down. And when you got through, you'd get to hold the handle of the other side and pull it and shut. It cost him some money. Well, I went through that thing. I knew how to do it. I went through it fine. It tickled me to death to get through all them things. The first one I ever went through. I saw them work, but never went through one. When I got up to his house, his house was a swale. Full of water. They filled it up with straw and manure. Over here was a barn, over here was a house. And this spot out here was a knoll. He kept his car there because it was free from being wet, you know. Nasty. Nasty. So I thought, well, I'll go through this swale and get over in that knoll.

speaker: William Morgan
words: So I went out and walked across this thing. I walked over there. It sounded all right. I drove my car and down she went. Down in the mud, clear down. Well, the men, there were six nine -horse teams... ...plowing in the field next door. Not next. And they were just unhitchy. So I waited to let him come and I flagged down a team of horses. They had eveners there and they hooked onto my car and pulled it out and over onto this knoll. And it helped him. I offered to pay them and they wouldn't take any money. And that's one thing they used to do. Very few, if you'd done a man accommodation, usually they didn't take any money for it. I never... I talked to them. They put their horses away. Here comes Smith with a load of whiskey. Permits. And he was about half shot and his head foreman was with him and he was about half shot. And the woman took. She wasn't quite so bad, but she was bad.

speaker: William Morgan
words: So... June came up to this gate. Smith. And he was drunk enough that he was bragging about what a fine deal it was and he was showing this foreman of his how to do it. He had a Franklin air -cooled automobile. Cost him those days four thousand dollars. That's an expensive car, them days. He'd ratchet up theirs as he'd been in the field. And as he's gone along, for some reason it caught or he didn't get a hold of it just right. And he pulled this down and he was trying to pull this, forgot about the car going forward. He was right through that gate and this man said, okay.

speaker: William Morgan
words: Oh, God, his foreman, kid had even laughed. The more he laughed, the more mad this fella got. Didn't hurt the car a bit. Never broke the windshield. Never done a darn thing to him. Never scratched it. So he felt pretty good about that he could build another gate, but he couldn't buy another automobile. So he drove down to the house, coming this way. And there was my car sitting over in the snow. So this goddamn foreman, he says, aw, Smith, he says, you're a goddamn very Franklin.

speaker: William Morgan
words: Why this damn old cattle truck here? He says, why ask?

speaker: William Morgan
words: I'll bet you that Ford, better car than this one, why it got through this mud hole and drove over there. It drove right through there. Oh, they just like to take it, he was needle. He says, this car will go anywhere where a Ford will.

speaker: William Morgan
words: Down he went. Took six head of horses to pull up there. Frankly, they weighed 4 ,000 pounds. That thing was clear into the running boards in mud. It was in a mess. They took six head of horses, they had to go and harness up six head of horses, get the eveners off the plows.

speaker: William Morgan
words: But they don't have to pull it out. Sounds like he had a pretty rough day for himself.

speaker: William Morgan
words: I want to tell you something. I wasn't pomper. No way. And he was another one of these fellas that wouldn't have anything to do with a peddler. But I was in the house.

speaker: William Morgan
words: So, Cook introduced me to him. He said, this man here, Mr. So -and -so. I says, Mr. Smith, I'd like to arrange the stay overnight with you and I'll pay for my meals and my lodging. Same price I pay Frank Henley that runs the Umrah Hotel. I always smart enough to know that you call a man by his first name, a full name if you can. Well, Frank Henley, his father was a grain buyer, but Frank, his son, run the hotel there. I says, whatever Frank charges for his room and meals, I'll pay you. Well, he says, I never turned a man down in my life. I says, I guess it's all right.

speaker: William Morgan
words: What's your business?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Oh, I says, I sell stuff a little bit, but that has nothing to do with this. Well, he says, dinner's about ready, so come on, set him with us. And I said, I'm selling, I sell groceries house to house. Well, he set me at the end of the table and the crew sat here. He just sat down here. There was about four feet difference between all the vegetables and everything you had to eat. I had nothing in front of my, just a plate.

speaker: William Morgan
words: Well, they just about finished dinner before they started passing me anything. I didn't say a word, just sat there and took it. They finally passed me what was left of each plate. I guess he told them to do that, I don't know. Anyhow, I ate for dinner, if nothing had happened. Picked up, got up, picked up the paper and they had a paper out of Spokane. Went over in the corner and sat down. So they were all drinking and they passed the whiskey around to these fellows driving the plows. And they all got to feel pretty good today, so I never pulled it.

speaker: William Morgan
words: And they sat down. Oh, I guess the poker game had gone on at least an hour.

speaker: William Morgan
words: Liz Smith turned around and says, well, peddler, whatever your name is, he says, why don't you join us?

speaker: William Morgan
words: I said, I don't know nothing about poker. Playing cards, I said, I don't play cards.

speaker: William Morgan
words: Well, you got any money?

speaker: William Morgan
words: I said, oh, not very much like you guys have. I probably got, oh, maybe a hundred dollars. I knew they didn't have much money, among all of them.

speaker: William Morgan
words: He says, you got a hundred dollars with you?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Well, I says, when you're paying your expenses like I do, meals, lodging, over the country, you got to keep pretty much cash on hand.

speaker: William Morgan
words: He says, well, what do you sell anyhow?

speaker: William Morgan
words: I says, coffee and tea and syrup and baking powder. Stuff is used here. Well, I never bought from a peddler in my life. I says, well, I don't blame you. Well, I says, I stopped at the bank this afternoon and found out that June Smith was pretty well thought over on this territory. I says, you run about twelve hundred acres of land here.

speaker: William Morgan
words: Well, what did you do left for? I said, well, I wonder if you have that or what?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Well, I said, well, I'm here, John Shaw from Seattle is my boss. He hired me to go out here and sell and I just hung out at the Good Ones because our sales policies are different than you ever heard. I says, if I did sell you a bill of groceries, I wouldn't send it to COD. You have to pay these other peddlers. I just ship it to you. Open bill of lading.

speaker: William Morgan
words: And, does the bill suit you?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Here's my address, phone number. I live in Clarkson. I'll come and get it.

speaker: William Morgan
words: Well, I says, you've been selling lately?

speaker: William Morgan
words: I says, well, just since the first year. I says, I just got out of the army. I was overseas. Germany.

speaker: William Morgan
words: You in the army?

speaker: William Morgan
words: I told him the regiment, show me where I'd been.

speaker: William Morgan
words: I didn't tell him that I played poker in the army all the time. Not that I played day and night. I had enough money to go to southern France. I was down on the Riviera. I was in the casino at Monte Carlo. I had all the money and I got transferred into the band the last month of the war because I loaned the lieutenant of the band poker money. I had all the money. He transferred it into the band.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that his way of paying you back?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Well, partially, yes.

speaker: William Morgan
words: But I was in the infantry before that. I got shot at there. But I got transferred in the band and the first time I played, they gave me a trombone. I got down out in the woods there and handed me some of these army teams that's hard to play, like bombasto. Some of them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Like what?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Bombasto and Under the Devil Eel and Stars and Stripes Forever and all those army pieces.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Have you played before?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Of course. I played in the Lucent Band. Well, I didn't make a very good impression. I didn't play too good. But anyhow, since I'd been transferred, they didn't bother taking me back. They were sending me back, disqualifying me. So I stayed in the army band. But I told him about it. And, well, come on and play. I says, I don't know how to play poker like you fellas do. Hell, you know how to play poker. I called him my first name. I says, June, you know over in Lucent, you're kind of feared over there. He'd been playing some big stuff over here in Lucent. I found that out in Pomeroy. And I called him my name and hit him like this. I says, you got kind of a bad name over there in Lucent. I said, the boys are afraid of you. Well, you know, that's just like putting soap on a fella. That soaped him up pretty much.

speaker: William Morgan
words: Well, he says, you're not a bad fella. I don't know who in the hell you are. But by God, he says, you don't seem to be so bad. Come on, play with us. Well, I got to have money left. I might lose some. I tell you what I'll do. Whatever I've got in my pocket, I'll put up half of it. I'll have to keep the rest for meals. Still on that meal and lodging business. I figured you wouldn't charge me anything. You didn't either. Next morning. Well, I got into this poker game. And I'd ask the silliest questions. I got three of one color and two of that. I didn't mean anything. I'm going to send you a deal.

speaker: William Morgan
words: Wound up. I had the money. $750.

speaker: William Morgan
words: Well, the cook come in and said, you've just got to work. You just got to be called off. So she made him quit. He gave me a room upstairs. I heard him talking that they were going to take that money away from me. So this woman says, you leave that fella alone. He's a big fella. He's not such a dumbbell as you might think he is. He says, I watched him. He's no dumbbell. She said, you better leave him alone. You're all drunk and he's not. I bet you he'd whip the whole bunch of them if he had to. He's right out of the army too, right where they kill people. So you leave him alone until in the morning. So they breathe through it. Thank God for that because I'm taking chairs and barricaded the door. Well, this is the best bluff I ever played in playing poker. I came down next morning. They were just sitting down at the breakfast table. I didn't sit down. I said, I'm out of here. I said, I'll... I got to straighten this game up last night. I got into a game here and maybe shouldn't have. I got into a game here and maybe shouldn't have. I got into a game here and maybe shouldn't have. I said, I'm going to give it back. I said, I want to know each one of you fellas how much you lost. I said, you man over there had only your name.

speaker: William Morgan
words: How much did you lose last night?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Well, he said, I lost about $35.

speaker: William Morgan
words: I'm about $35 later down there.

speaker: William Morgan
words: I said, how much did you lose?

speaker: William Morgan
words: About the third time, Smith was sitting on the end there. He jumped up. No goddamn pendant can come in here and make fun of me. I said, I haven't even spoke to you yet. I never made fun of you.

speaker: William Morgan
words: What did I say?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Well, you didn't say nothing, but you're giving this money back to these people. That ain't the way you play poker. Well, I know most of that, but I didn't win it fair.

speaker: William Morgan
words: Well, he says, why didn't you?

speaker: William Morgan
words: I said, because you was all drunk and I wasn't. I wasn't drunk. I knew what I was doing. Well, you said you didn't know how to play poker. Well, I don't. Not with you. I said, you're supposed to be one of the best poker players in this country. I wouldn't try to play against you under normal circumstances. I said, I'll play a little poker for the army, yes. Just die in quarters and dollars and a few things like that.

speaker: William Morgan
words: He says, sit down. I want to talk to this man. So I sit down. He turned to this fellow.

speaker: William Morgan
words: How much did you lose last night?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Well, he was something, I don't know, a hundred or something. Over a hundred. Well, I guess I'm the biggest loser. The current baby asks each one of them. Well, unless they're wrong. You'll get all this back. They'll pay you. Not that damn piddler. I'm not going to let him come in my house and make fun of me.

speaker: William Morgan
words: Now he says, kid, you put that back in your pocket. You shut up. Don't tell anybody either.

speaker: William Morgan
words: What happened?

speaker: William Morgan
words: That's the way you want it. That's the way you want it. That's the part that tickled the deck.

speaker: William Morgan
words: I started right out of the tickler. That's the best bluff I ever made in playing poker.

speaker: William Morgan
words: I told that once, I've told that a hundred times and then some.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that if you hadn't done that, that they would have taken it from you back in the morning?

speaker: William Morgan
words: No. I don't think they would. I don't think they would. But I got, he says, you stay here. As soon as I get the boys out on the plows, I want to talk to you. He gave me an order. The biggest order I ever got in Garfield County. It took six headed horses on a wagon to haul it out from Pumley. Oh, I don't believe a word of it. Yes, you do.

speaker: William Morgan
words: You're a big brother to Ed Smith, don't you?

speaker: William Morgan
words: That's what I'm talking about.

speaker: William Morgan
words: You know, he's a brother too, don't you Ed? You know Ed too, don't you?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Yeah.

speaker: William Morgan
words: Well, I'll tell you, I laugh about that yet because it's just one thing that happens. It just don't happen. But it did. He gave me an order from 52.

speaker: Unknown Speaker
words: One down and how many of these are up?

speaker: William Morgan
words: These are all up. The next one up, there you go. I'll take this down and you can put this back.

speaker: Unknown Speaker
words: He gave me an order from what?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Well, we sold the barrel holes, 50 gallon barrels of syrup. They made syrup as yet, so I sold him a 50 gallon barrel of syrup. I also sold one to Bill Bozarth out here at Cuddesite too. And I had three or four orders of barrels of syrup. You don't hear of anything like that now.

speaker: William Morgan
words: I wonder what made him such a big order.

speaker: William Morgan
words: I mean, I don't know if I would say it was home. I told him he was a feared man in Lewiston. Played poker, that's one thing. Another thing, I said I'd went to the bank. I did that. And he was rated very good. That I had to do. And several other things. He wasn't one of these fellas who wanted to be outdone.

speaker: William Morgan
words: And he was making good money. He was making big money those days. We got up to about two and a half a bushel that year.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: About what year was that?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Well, that was in 19... When we got back from the war. Oh, it was right at the end of the war. 18, 19, 18, 19. And then 20 was the crash next year. And he went with it. He went with it too in 21. Lost his farm. Because he was a good spender. Sounds like. Well, that's...

speaker: William Morgan
words: That's a hell of a good story. Well, it's a fact. Absolutely. You don't even take my word for it. There's a man in there. He's a good spender.

speaker: William Morgan
words: Well, that's a funny thing that's happened.

speaker: William Morgan
words: I had one more thing and then I'll quit. Down at Dodge Service Station.

speaker: William Morgan
words: You know where Dodge is down on the other side of Pomeroy?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Yeah. Well, the old man Dodge built that big house right along the highway there. Left hand side. He ran a lot of sheep. And grain. So, it was about 11 o 'clock in the morning. Wind was blowing colder than the devil. And he was outside in front, between the house and the highway, digging a ditch to bury some water pipe. And connect up his house. Well, I come in there. Told him what I was doing. And the funny part of it was, he liked whiskey. Oh boy. Peddlers come in there and sold him 10 cases of whiskey. And they delivered to him right out of their car. And they had him sampling. When they opened the trunk of the car, why they would stretch over and they knew where these bottles were. And they'd give him a bottle right out of a case. And they opened another case and give him another one. They did give him whiskey. But all the rest was tea. So, I told him I was selling whiskey.

speaker: William Morgan
words: You want to tell me you got that?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Hey, good deal.

speaker: William Morgan
words: You know what's that?

speaker: William Morgan
words: I said, well, I'm not that kind of a fool. I told him who I was. I said, I'd like to stay for dinner.

speaker: William Morgan
words: What's happening now?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Well,

speaker: William Morgan
words: What you got anything, Greg, with you?

speaker: William Morgan
words: I said, I got some Jamaican ginger.

speaker: William Morgan
words: You got some Jamaican ginger? With you?

speaker: William Morgan
words: I can't sell to you, though.

speaker: William Morgan
words: Well, why?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Well, I said, it's 96 % alcohol.

speaker: William Morgan
words: You mean Jamaican ginger is 96 % alcohol?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Yes. But I can't sell to you. You're not going to buy it from me.

speaker: William Morgan
words: Well, he says, how will I get some of it?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Well, I can ship you. Order of groceries. If you want this Jamaican ginger for your horses, that's all right.

speaker: William Morgan
words: How much could I buy?

speaker: William Morgan
words: I said, well, I'll sell you a gallon. Well, come on in the house.

speaker: William Morgan
words: Now, where's your sample of that Jamaican ginger?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Well, I had a case. I figured, well, anyhow, the tubes are about that long. About that big. Big as ginger. Probably, oh, a good half ounce. Well, probably an ounce or two.

speaker: William Morgan
words: And I brought this sample case in. And he says, let's see it. I said, now you can't drink that. That's not for drink. That's Jamaican ginger. Hot, burning. I can drink anything. Gasoline, coal oil, you just name it. I said, you can't drink that. But that time he picked this tube out there and he just pulled the cork and down it went. And I thought it killed a man. Jesus Christ almighty. He coughed, he run over and he grabbed the dipper and he wants to drink five or six dippers of water. And he gagged and he struggled and he held on to the sink and it just burned the hell out of his face.

speaker: William Morgan
words: Did you ever taste Jamaican ginger?

speaker: William Morgan
words: No. Well, pure alcohol is a hundred. Well, pure alcohol is two hundred. But as we know it, it's a hundred.

speaker: William Morgan
words: Well, he coughed and he spit and the tears ran out of his eyes and he finally leaned over against the wall. And out of that water it diluted that ginger to a certain extent. And pretty soon the young man, he says, I have a damn good notion to give you a whooping. I says, now, the first place you can't do it. I've never been, nobody ever gave me a whooping yet. Not about to now, but I said, let's forget that now. I don't want to, I won't fight you. No consideration. I told you that you couldn't drink it. I told you to leave it alone.

speaker: William Morgan
words: Well, isn't that right?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Well, yes. Well, yes, I guess you was right. Well, okay, I guess it was my fault.

speaker: William Morgan
words: So, we had dinner. After dinner you give me a hell of a good order, including the galleys of Jamaican ginger.

speaker: William Morgan
words: And I says, now, God damn you, why don't you try to use that as a trade?

speaker: William Morgan
words: He says, no, no, I had nothing. It's a little bit of gold. I'll take your word for it now.

speaker: William Morgan
words: What was it usually used for, Jamaican ginger?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Well, I never did find out. Actually, I never did know what that was for. I do know it. They used it in the moonshine, because it had a whiskey color. I had several customers who bought gallons. One of them was a bachelor up here at Gifford. He lived out there by John Black's place, right behind it there. And he had bought a gallon every year, and he made moonshine. And I don't suppose that in a gallon of moonshine, I don't suppose he used over one ounce, maybe, or maybe two. But that stuff is hot. Oh, God. And I was in Jamaica three years ago, and I wanted to see some Jamaican ginger. The fella rode out on that little barricade railroad from...

speaker: William Morgan
words: What's the bay there? Kingston?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Kingston is the main city. Well, that's Kingston, no. Kingston is way down east. No, you land there at the bay.

speaker: William Morgan
words: What's that bay?

speaker: William Morgan
words: It's where all the airframes lay. Anyhow, the railroad runs from this bay, not Mobile Bay. Well, anyhow, from the bay up to North, where they have these bauxite factories. And the biggest supply of bauxite in the world is there in Jamaica. And that's where most all our aluminum comes from. I think about 75 % of it comes out of Jamaica. Well, I rode out on that train in that big rum factory, English rum, Jamaica rum. So we went into the factory that makes that. So they give me a sample of pure rum. Don't try. They give us a drop on a little, I don't know what it was now, kind of a little spooky steel. Just about one drop. Now it says don't try to swallow it, just put it under your jaw. That's the teeth. You'll get the flavor.

speaker: William Morgan
words: I took that one drop, put it in there. I got it just that quick, completely numb. I couldn't feel nothing. The whole time it was there. Jesus Christ, I never saw anything like that. I often wondered what would happen if a man would take a swallow that must have killed him. Well, your friend at down by Parma right there, he would have done it probably if he had given him a chance. He would have been a damn fool. You know, there's one thing I want to ask you.

speaker: William Morgan
words: You know, when you're talking about staying in the U .S.

speaker: William Morgan
words: and going and staying overnight with people and that kind of thing, would that really help bring about friendships with people?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Well, they were tickled to death to talk to me. Everywhere I went, people would say, no more. I heard about you down in so -and -so. Come on in. Well, it isn't over yet. Yeah, I had no trouble. I lived in the country. I never went to town maybe once in two or three weeks. And I never paid very near as a real needle.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They wouldn't take it, right?

speaker: William Morgan
words: No, that's right. Because that was a good conversation. I played football here for the high school, basketball, baseball. And I had known the different kinds of horses they worked, whether they were Clydesdales or Perchons or Shires or Cayuses or whatever they might be. I knew the different kinds of grain, what kinds of raisin, turkey red. I told them about working on the thrashing outfit. How I hated to work in this red russian because it made you itch on the skin. I didn't talk anything in the farms, you see. And then I had lots of gadgets I used to do. Just little silly things. I had a tube about that long. And I filled it with syrup. Crystal white syrup. Clear. And you leave an air space about that far, you know, at the top. Turn it upside down and that bubble would go up like a balloon. You've done that probably. No, but I know what you're saying. But I had little kids laying around saying, Come over here, sonny, I'll show you something. I want to see a balloon go up. And I turned that thing up and I turned up quick to see that balloon shake, tear drop. Go at the top.

speaker: William Morgan
words: Little kids that get the biggest kick out of that?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Just silly little things.

speaker: William Morgan
words: And you had all that together and I never did it. I was in trouble and I never did it. It never was turned out. Never had any trouble at all. Big orders. Lots of big ones because our neighbors had bought from the year before. And I liked the soap and the starch.

speaker: William Morgan
words: And again, salesmanship was one of the best professions on earth if you use a drive.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What kind of shape were most of the families in at that time, like in the early 20s?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Terrible.

speaker: William Morgan
words: You said most of them did. Terrible. God. No toilets. No logging water lots of times. No sanitation. I'd hate to go back those days. There was lots of good ones too. Not percentage wise. Lots of nice big homes. Even those big homes weren't modern. Take the big house over at Matt Beale's place right next to June Smith's. Ten rooms. Big ones. Three stories.

speaker: William Morgan
words: No accommodations in it. No heat upstairs.

speaker: William Morgan
words: I'd hate to have to go back.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it hard on the families, you think, to be comfortable in their houses then?

speaker: William Morgan
words: No, they didn't know any different. They lived that way. Most of them. They didn't know what luxury it was like we live here now. No, they just didn't. They ate their meals. One of the directors of the Farmers United States Bank in Pumaroi never had a table or a chair where they ate.

speaker: William Morgan
words: I ate the apple boxes.

speaker: William Morgan
words: Just ordinary wooden boxes they got from the stores. And they had a couple of three boards laid on some boxes for a table. And yet, he was the director of the American Bank. That's black. He had four sons. They all graduated from Washington State University.

speaker: William Morgan
words: They basked, all of them. You can go look at their records. Their home or something like that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about the women?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Was it hard on the wives to be... Sure.

speaker: William Morgan
words: They lived. Seemed to live just as good or better than any of the modern ones. Here's Mrs. Ita Bache, over in Clarkson now. She's 83. I'm 81. And I called on her when she was on the farm. Sold her groceries. Her husband got killed and a horse killed him some way in a barn there. And she's a good healthy woman right now. She's 85. She's 85. My daughter and her children, her two daughters, were raised right along... ...with Mrs. Bache next door. And we call her Grandma. She's just part of the family. Grandma. Every time they have a birthday or Christmas, New Year's, Grandma's always invited.

speaker: William Morgan
words: She's related to half of the Calfield County over there.

speaker: William Morgan
words: Grand person. She's had a hard time too in the early days. No, you take the way we used to live. Up here at the county sac. In a place where one day... Front room and a house. Oh yeah. No floor in it. Just dirt floor.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The stove was settling down?

speaker: William Morgan
words: It was in the kitchen and it had settled down. This woman had swept out in front of the stove until it was about 18 inches lower than the sills. Just a hole in the ground. And she'd poured water in there and grease and stuff until that was solid. Just a solid kick of dirt. But it was smooth. And she stepped down in that hole to cook. You wouldn't believe that. The name Shoemaker. George Shoemaker. He lived about a mile and a half this side of the mission up here. You wouldn't believe those things. I saw it. I was in the kitchen myself. I sold them a bill of goods for their thrash market. Yes sir. But he was one of these lower type Germans that believed nothing but money. Possession. His family. Nothing. But his son, he was headed to a trucking outfit down here. He started raking me. I didn't lose him. Dead before.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you learn anything?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Yes, I can tell you a lot of stuff. I went house to house here for seven years. I learned something. I'll tell you.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it much different in the city, in Winston, than it was out in the country?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Oh yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I mean as far as the way people would have been?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Oh yes. Yeah, in town here. Pretty much. It's not modernized like we are now, but then they had nice places. Heat in one or two rooms. Heaters and so on. Water. Hot water.

speaker: William Morgan
words: They were pretty nice in town. You live in Tylen they were called city dudes. They live in the city. They were I suppose really better than old poor farmer.

speaker: William Morgan
words: That's what I heard. I heard they acted like they were too. That's right. And they said the country people were pumpkin rollers and you know, hicks and smelled bad. That's true.

speaker: William Morgan
words: They didn't have water to like hot water all through the day. That's right. Hard work. They come to town of course they didn't really have too much in them. They did dress better, lots better. And they had jobs and they had a salary coming in and maybe a little one every month. But they didn't actually live longer and they didn't enjoy themselves any more than these farmers did. They'd go out hunting and fishing and things like that.

speaker: William Morgan
words: And their meals were pretty much wholesome. They'd kill a beef. They knew how to take care of it. Smoke it. Dry it. My coach used to kill three or four hogs at a time. Grind the meat up into sausage. My mother would stay two or three days at a time cooking sausage, frying sausage on the stove. Put it in a five gallon stone crocks covered with grease. Seal it. Keep it just as nice as the refrigerator.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How good was the food then when you were at these places in the country staying over there?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Generally good.

speaker: William Morgan
words: Generally good. Fried potatoes. Carrots. Parsips. Turnips. Corn. Dried corn. Canned fruit. Lots of canned fruit. Hell, they canned in way more than they do now. People are too damn lazy now. And the young girls, they learned to cook. These young girls now between 18 and 30, they don't. Okay. I don't know anything.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would you say that cooking was better in the country than it was in the city?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Better food. Yeah. Yes, sir. I like old country fried chicken.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you want to have? Country gravy?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Sure.

speaker: William Morgan
words: Country sausage?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Sure. Yes, sir. I raised on the farm. We had lots of sausage. Lots of meat.

speaker: William Morgan
words: We'd get our meat. We usually wait until the first meal would freeze and we'd kill a beef. Divide it up with the neighbors. Maybe three of the neighbors come in, they'd each take a quarter. Each one of them. They'd kill one and we'd go over there and get a quarter.

speaker: William Morgan
words: Why do it that way? Because you only use a quarter at a time?

speaker: William Morgan
words: That's right. We didn't want the spot.

speaker: William Morgan
words: We didn't want fresh meat. You had to work more, you had to travel more, you had to spend more time. But you had good meat. Maybe better than what you're getting right now.

speaker: William Morgan
words: Grain fed, usually. I think we had actually better food.

speaker: William Morgan
words: You're talking about this, you're talking about the difference between country and town, city people.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that the country people were, felt real aware of that when they came into Lewiston State?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Yeah, I think so.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They felt out of place?

speaker: William Morgan
words: I think so. To a certain extent, yes.

speaker: William Morgan
words: I did. My city cousins were stuck up. They didn't. They weren't. They were just as common as us. But they didn't appear so. Of course, we lived without a part. Part of it in town, part of it in the country. We lived in both places at once. Farmed outside, lived in town. As I told you before, my dad was a stock buyer. So we moved to town in the wintertime. And right as we got rid of our stock, sometimes we'd leave them on the ranch and dad would go out here every day and take care of them every other day. Maybe go out and stay three or four days at the ranch house.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why did you live in town? What made it more convenient to be in town?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Oh!

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did she get a chance to socialize in town?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Not too much.

speaker: William Morgan
words: After we got into town, complications set in between her and her dad. They finally separated. No particular reason. Well, one reason was we got drinking too much for the whole thing. This one thing went on doing that here. They finally decided to separate.

speaker: William Morgan
words: No particular reason why, as near as I could ever find out. Other than they just didn't like each other after a while. I was about 16.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he go back out to the farm? Me? Did he?

speaker: William Morgan
words: No, father.

speaker: William Morgan
words: Well, a sad part of it, as the report says, he sold the ranch, his part of the ranch, half a little. He got the money and he was doped. Whatever happened to him, nobody knows. I never did. He left town. Never heard 20 cents.

speaker: William Morgan
words: So us kids had it pretty tough for about four years. Really.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were you the oldest?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So your other brothers were younger than you?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Yeah.

speaker: William Morgan
words: They were all four in here at one time. All together in this business. My youngest brother owned a pickle plant. The largest pickling plant in the country. There was no adversity.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: At that time, did your work support your brothers at all?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Not very much.

speaker: William Morgan
words: Each one of them made his own way. The only time that I did help my third brother was

speaker: William Morgan
words: Well, this is quite a story. My second brother had a lot of friends like I do. And he worked for one of them out here in the Lewis and Orchards. And beans were quite a thing in this country then. So my brother wanted to go to college. So his fellow says, well, if you want to raise a crop of beans, maybe you can sell them for enough to go to college. So he gave him his plow and horses and everything. My brother was working for him on the ranch. As soon as you get through the farm work, for me, well, you take a team and go out and plow your yard and your garden or acreage and plant your beans. Which he did. 20 acres. He harvested them just as soon as they were possible to be harvested. Brought them down here at this mill, just where Sambo's is now, and sold them there for $8 .50.

speaker: William Morgan
words: Next week, beans dropped and never did stop dropping. It went clear down to $3 .00 and $4 .00. But he got his 8 .50 and put him through two years at WSU. Two years. Had enough money to carry him through school two years. And then all of us always worked all summer. In the farms, farm thrashing, and everything else. All hard labor. All four brothers.

speaker: William Morgan
words: That's what Sambo and the others did.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were they working during that work those years after your father was gone? Were they working all that time too?

speaker: William Morgan
words: No, they weren't in school. They stayed with Mark.

speaker: William Morgan
words: In the summertime, yes, they worked hard. One of them worked on a pipeline. And the Silt and Clarkson fell off of that. It got hurt. However, he didn't recover from any damage. But my second brother, he had this money from the beans. And then he worked out on the harvest field and picked cherries. It used to be a big job, we had this territory picking cherries. And us boys had our places where we picked cherries and they always give us a job back. Every year, just this far. I have at that time though, was selling, so I didn't get to pick much. Just in the evenings or Sunday or something.

speaker: William Morgan
words: My brother, second brother, Lill, he was a partner with me here after. They all come in as me after I started this place. My second brother, Lill, was the top man at WSU. Graduation. You can check his records there if you want to. L .J. Morgan. He got offered a deanship to the Ellensburg normal. He didn't take it, he came with me. And my third brother, I'm sorry to tell you, he worked in the store this way. He didn't have any money and he wanted to go to school, so he stayed out of school one year. And he worked in different places, made all the money he could. As soon as he came with me and worked at the store, I told him if you work for me, you know nothing, I'll give you room and board.

speaker: William Morgan
words: And if the store don't make it, I'll go out and work and labor and pay your bill in college. So he graduated. That was the deal. If I couldn't make enough money in the store to pay his way through college, I would go out and work and labor. Any labor I get to pay through college.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you feel you needed him that bad or did you just want to help him?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Well, the deal was that neither him nor I would ever take a nickel out of that store. All the assets were left there. Unless we went to local bread, we took it out of our money and if we had it, he'd have paid for it. He'd done the same thing. We stayed out here.

speaker: William Morgan
words: But when it was time to go to college, that store was making all kinds of money. That's why both of them put teaching and came to me. One of them brothers got me incorporated. The fourth brother graduates from high school. He didn't go to college.

speaker: William Morgan
words: He started working for me. The other brother went up to run our store at Kindred Hill. He stayed there a year and wanted to come back to town so I gave him a pickle plant. One of them pickled a little pack. He run that thing for his whole life. It's the only job he ever had.

speaker: William Morgan
words: When did you first start the store as opposed to the...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that right after?

speaker: William Morgan
words: At that time you stopped doing the selling for Seattle.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the idea there? Did you figure that you were better?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Well, the wheat went down from two and a half to two bits of bushel. You couldn't sell those farmers anything. You couldn't even give it to them.

speaker: William Morgan
words: And our benefactor had all this land out here that would furnish the team and the horses for my brother to raise the beans. He lost everything he had. And he came down and says, Bill, I hate to crawl on my aunts and nieces. Give me a job.

speaker: William Morgan
words: I never had a cent there to speak of. He'd lost everything. So he went to work for me in the grocery store. Two years before that, I was a little tired of it. Oh, yeah, it is charged. It is charged. It was worth over $50 ,000. In two years time, I wiped him out. Of course, that was the time when all these farmers got wiped out. Lots of them. They come up here in Webb Ridge.

speaker: William Morgan
words: Parish had a farm out there that was worth $50 ,000. He sold it for more than that. They bought, they sold him a flour mill at Kendrick. And they lost that. And his entire money. I think he's on welfare right now.

speaker: William Morgan
words: That's just one. There's hundreds of them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did it all happen that fast? 20 and 21 in two years?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Yes, sir. Ever been it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it beans and everything else that all went down at once?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Sure.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Who had anything to buy the one? Do you have anything else?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Sure. Peas, a dollar and a half, two dollars and a hundred pounds of green beans. Dried beans.

speaker: William Morgan
words: They got three dollars and a quarter. $2 .85, they thought they had a mint. When Bob McGregor was appointed by President Roosevelt to represent the pea growers of the North Pacific, he went back to Washington, D .C. and said they needed five dollars and 85 cents a hundred for their peas.

speaker: William Morgan
words: And them buggers back there, they didn't know a pea from a rock. So they granted him a Bob McGregor from North scenario. A place right up the end of the street. That's the McGregor warehouse. And they owned a lot of land. All up in the upper country. And he went back and represented the pea growers. And those college professors, they didn't know what they were doing. And they gave him $5 .85, subsidized. And the whole Moscow, Genesee, Uniontown, Colt, here, Pomeroy, Garfield, California. Boy, when it started, it started.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that about in 35 in there?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Not long in there, yeah. Well, you can find it on the books up there, just exactly.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But then when it hit bottom in early 2021, didn't a lot of people stay?

speaker: William Morgan
words: I mean, man.

speaker: William Morgan
words: Hang on to their places. Up there they did, I know. Yeah, sure, all over. A lot of them did. They didn't mortgage it. They didn't have any money. They couldn't go to town. They couldn't do nothing. They just stayed there and ate their beef or potatoes or whatever they had and let her go at that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did that go on clear through the 20s or did it pick up again?

speaker: William Morgan
words: No, it picked up when Roosevelt was elected in 32. And then things started on the, well, the first year didn't start second year. So it was 10 years, we're talking 10, 12 years.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So that whole 20s was depression for the farmers?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Pretty much, yes. But not for like the people in Lewiston, though, the same way it was for the people in town.

speaker: William Morgan
words: No.

speaker: William Morgan
words: We had cattle, we had hogs, we had lumber.

speaker: William Morgan
words: Lots of lumber. People jockeyed around. They traded labor and managed to get by. I don't know, during that time I was making money. I started the store in 1921 with $400 even. That was my total money. I bought the store, the fixtures, stock, and the bale of people to the mouse. I bought a whole caboodle for $400.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was the name of the store you bought?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Morgan's Grocery.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you bought it, whose place?

speaker: William Morgan
words: A fellow by the name of Littler. He had this little store. I borrowed the money from my father, my father -in -law, my wife's father.

speaker: William Morgan
words: We didn't take a dime out of that store. Three years. Not a dime. That's what these fellows don't do now. They think of themselves, nobody else. I wouldn't accept a bill, wouldn't let anyone run any bills. My wife was a graduate nurse. She was in charge of the White's Hospital. I don't know if you heard that. She made enough money being head -nursed to pay our living. Whatever profits were made in the store, which remained there. In 1924, I showed a profit on an $8 ,307 stock of $9 ,700. It was enough to buy a quarter section of land. Now let's see anybody, any store you name, Albertson, Safeway, you show me one that can make enough profit to buy a quarter section of land today. Now you're traveling around, you just try it. You go up to Moscow, Poland, you find any store you pick, Yellow Front, Rosar's, I don't care what you say. See if that store makes enough money to buy a quarter section of land in a year.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, could you do it then? How'd you do it? How'd you manage it then?

speaker: William Morgan
words: I made it so I told everybody I knew more about groceries than anybody in the country. And I bought and sold certain things. One of these is malt, and I was telling you about 50 -gallon barrels.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It moves pretty fast, huh?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Yeah.

speaker: William Morgan
words: I think a 5 -gallon can of malt cost me $1 .15, and I think I sold it for $7 .50. That's two days wages. That quick.

speaker: William Morgan
words: Prunes?

speaker: William Morgan
words: I bought a ton of dried prunes from Henry in Juliaetta, I've been hearing about dryers. I'm out from making moonshine. I guess I'm going to be proving it. Don't make a difference whether I did or not. It's illegal to sell prunes. It's not illegal to make moonshine. But I presume that's where it went.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you have any idea what quantities of that malt and hops you're moving at?

speaker: William Morgan
words: Not now, no.

speaker: William Morgan
words: Too big of an ounce. But then you could buy things. For instance, Mason -Erman had a big warehouse down here. They came over to see me one day about the stone crust. That's why I'm still in the crop business. I got all sizes of crust. I bought their entire stock for two bits a gallon. Less 50%. Freight paid. In other words, I paid $12 .50 for a gallon of crock. On the five gallon crock it cost me $0 .62.

speaker: William Morgan
words: Today we sell a five gallon crock for $15 .60.

speaker: William Morgan
words: That's what you call inflation.

speaker: William Morgan
words: Yeah, I should get going.
